fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

GILL J

These proceedings concern competing applications for property adjustment between the husband and wife in the context of some 14 other respondents being joined to the proceedings. The broader context is that the husband is a failed property developer who conducted that business through many corporate entities, the liquidators for a number of which are joined to the proceedings. As will become apparent, the remnants of that business are without substance, to the extent that there will be a major shortfall in meeting the creditors’ claims.
Following the failure of that business the husband appears to have relocated to Country P from where he took part in the proceedings. There can be no expectation that he will return to Australia.
The wife and husband have three children together who are now aged between 6 and 11 years. They are in the full-time care of the wife in Australia. This hearing did not involve parenting issues.
The balance of the parties is constituted by various lawyers who have acted for the wife who are pursuing claims, and security for claims, against the wife.

Schedule of parties

The schedule below sets out a complete list of the parties to the litigation identifying those that participated, those that discontinued, those that filed a submitting notice and those that failed to participate at all.
Applicant – Ms Pirani - Wife.
First respondent – Mr Pirani - Husband.
Second respondent – B Pty Ltd (ACN [...])
Third respondent – C Pty Ltd (ACN [...]) - no attendance at the final hearing.
Fourth respondent - D1 Pty Ltd (ACN [...]) - filed a submitting notice.
Fifth respondent - D2 PTY LTD (ACN [...]).
Sixth respondent – E Pty Ltd (ACN [...]).
Seventh respondent – Ms OO/AO Lawyers –previous lawyer for the wife.
Eighth respondent – AF Pty Ltd (ACN [...]) - previous lawyer for the wife.
Ninth respondent – Mr AH.
Tenth respondent – AJ Pty Ltd – no attendance at the final hearing.
Eleventh respondent – AL Limited – no attendance at the final hearing.
Twelfth respondent – AK Pty Ltd – filed a submitting notice.
Thirteenth respondent – AM Pty Ltd – discontinued proceedings.
Fourteenth respondent – ZZ Pty Ltd (ACN [...]) - previous lawyer for the wife.
Fifteenth respondent – AN Pty Ltd (ACN [...]).
Of the respondents who engaged with the proceedings (that is, excluding those who have filed a notice of discontinuance, a submitting notice or otherwise failed to participate) the principal protagonists were B Pty Ltd (second respondent), D2 Pty Ltd (fifth respondent) and E Pty Ltd (sixth respondent). These were subject to the same liquidator and were commonly represented. Together they will be described as the liquidator. The liquidator’s claim was primarily prosecuted for B Pty Ltd.
The ninth protagonist was Mr AH who had personally loaned monies to the husband prior to the failure of the husband’s businesses, with such monies being secured against the former matrimonial home located at Suburb G, New South Wales (“the [Suburb G] property”).
The claims pursued by the liquidator in these proceedings related to director’s loans made by B Pty Ltd to the husband that were secured by mortgage upon the Suburb G property. The liquidator accepted that the priority for this mortgage fell behind both that of a mortgage held by Westpac and the mortgage held by Mr AH (as described below). By the time of the trial the Suburb G property had been sold, with the proceeds, after the payment of relevant expenses and of the mortgages held by Westpac and by Mr AH, in the order of $3.9 million, being held pending further order.
Despite the husband, by means of his initial response document, apparently accepting such claims in general terms, and despite the husband reiterating that acceptance in his final amended response, when the wife formally accepted B Pty Ltd’s claims during the trial the husband repudiated such position, noting that previously he had challenged the mortgage and the quantum of the loans. Ultimately, he was not permitted to challenge the presence of the mortgage due to the manner of his conduct of the proceedings and his failure to plead such a challenge. He, however, contested the level of indebtedness secured by the mortgage, while the wife accepted the quantum claimed by the liquidator, which was of the order of $11,211,703.[1] Significantly, this debt would more than extinguish the remaining funds obtained from the sale of the Suburb G property.
By consent, Mr AH’s claim against Suburb G was recognised by the other impacted parties, as was his priority over them. Accordingly consent orders were made during the proceedings which will be reflected in the determination of the pool of property set out later in the judgment.
Those orders will mean that Westpac is paid from the proceeds of Suburb G, then Mr AH. The balance of those funds is then the subject of the, lately contentious, claim by the liquidator.
The balance of the active protagonists was made up of lawyers and/or law firms associated with the wife. These were constituted by a Ms OO and in her stead the firm AO Lawyers (the seventh respondent), and the corporate entity through which that entity practiced, AN Pty Ltd (the fifteenth respondent), who although are separate parties will be referred to together as Ms OO. The other two law firms were the AF Pty Ltd (the eighth respondent) and ZZ Pty Ltd (the fourteenth respondent).
Each of the law firms were engaged in the litigation for the purpose of either securing or receiving purported legal fees owed to them by the wife.

ORDERS SOUGHT

Land registration number [...], [Town AP], [City AQ], [Country P];

Unless otherwise specified in these orders:
Each party be solely entitled to the exclusion of the other to all other property and chattels of whatsoever nature and kind in the possession of such party as at the date of these orders and that for this purpose bank accounts are deemed to be in the possession of the person whose name appears on the banks’ record thereof, insurance policies are deemed to be in the possession of the beneficiary thereof and superannuation entitlements are deemed to be in the possession of the person who is named as the worker whose age or working future provides the conditions for payment out of such entitlements.

The husband (first respondent)

The husband’s position and orders sought in the proceedings changed substantially over the course of the proceedings. At times the position and orders sought were confusing or contradictory, sometimes in support of the wife and at other times rejecting her claims. The various positions and orders sought are set out below.
In the husband’s response on 29 June 2023, he sought video calls with the children, agreed to both spousal maintenance and child support orders, and for the Suburb G property to be sold with the balance of proceeds paid to him. He also sought that as an interim order $150,000 be paid to the wife’s solicitors for legal fees.
On 24 October 2023, the husband sought the application of the seventh respondent be dismissed, and for the jewellery to be provided to the wife, with restraints on dealing with it.
On 5 March 2024, the husband sought the children be placed on the Airport Watchlist, no longer sought orders for child support and spousal maintenance, and sought that the balance of proceeds from the Suburb G property be split evenly between the husband and the wife.
On 22 July 2024, the husband sought the Court determine the various claims on the Suburb G proceeds, and for the parties to participate in mediation in that regard.
On 21 October 2024, the husband sought shared custody of the children upon a return to Australia, challenged various alleged debts and mortgages owed, sought that the legal fees claimed by the seventh and eighth respondents be assessed and paid by the wife, and agreed to payments in relation to the Westpac and H Pty Ltd (no H Pty Ltd mortgage was identified in the proceedings) mortgages, and the debt owed to the ninth respondent.
On 28 February 2025, the husband sought that an order for $150,000 of partial property settlement to the wife and $450,000 of litigation funding to the wife be set aside. These orders were never met by the husband. He also sought the proceeds for the sale of various properties be determined according to final orders, and for the proceeds from sale of the jewellery be divided.
On 6 March 2025, the husband sought division of the proceeds of sale of the Suburb J and Suburb G properties. In relation to Suburb G, he sought the proceeds be entirely paid to himself, and for Suburb J, that the proceeds be split between the husband and the wife. He sought an order that the proceeds of the jewellery be split 50/50 between the husband and the wife.
In the husband’s final orders sought, he sought that the Suburb G and Suburb J properties be sold and split such that 50 per cent of the proceeds be paid to him. He also sought that the jewellery held by the wife be divided between the parties, that the wife pay her costs, that the wife’s assets be disclosed and divided between the parties, and for further disclosure from the wife.
Ultimately, the husband sought in his written closing submissions emailed to the court on 2 June 2025 the following orders:
That the property known as [QQ Street], [Suburb J], NSW be sold by judicial sale.

That the property known as [F Street], [Suburb G], NSW was sold and the proceeds added to the matrimonial pool.

That 50% of the net proceeds from the sale of both properties (after costs and liabilities) be paid to the First Respondent.

That the Wife pay her own legal costs personally, or that they be assessed under the Legal Profession Uniform Law (NSW).

That all jewellery purchased by the Husband during the marriage and retained by the Wife be declared matrimonial property.

That all bank accounts, investment funds, and financial holdings in the Wife’s name, accrued during the marriage, be disclosed and included in the asset pool.

That the Wife disclose all current bank balances and provide financial statements from the date of separation to present.

B Pty Ltd (the second respondent)

The second respondent’s Case Outline Document filed 12 March 2025 indicated an intent to file a Minute of Order “at the commencement of the final hearing when it is established if the applicant and or the respondent will file any evidence”.
The Minute of Order was ultimately contained within the closing submissions handed up during the course of the final hearing, marked as Exhibit R2-4. The orders sought relief only against the husband, being that Order 14 made on 7 June 2023 preventing B Pty Ltd from demanding payment be discharged, that the husband pay to the second respondent the sum of $9,590,724 by way of repayment of monies plus interest, and for the enforcement of a mortgage held over the Suburb G property.

C Pty Ltd (the third respondent) [No appearance at hearing]

In the third respondent’s response to final orders filed 14 March 2025, it sought that the applicant’s amended application filed 5 March 2025 be dismissed. It was also sought that Order 14 of the Orders made on 7 June 2023 be discharged, that the first respondent repay money owed to the second respondent and if contravention occurs that the second respondent be entitled to enforce a mortgage held to procure payment. Costs were also sought on an indemnity basis.

D1 Pty Ltd (the fourth respondent)

The fourth respondent, D1 Pty Ltd ACN [...], filed a submitting notice on 1 August 2024 and sought to submit to any order the Court may make in the proceedings and to be heard on the question of costs.

Ms OO/AO Lawyers (the seventh respondent) and AN Pty Ltd (the fifteenth respondent)

The seventh respondent sought a range of orders to assist with the recovery of alleged fees owed.
Per their Amended Case Outline filed 3 June 2025, the seventh and fifteenth respondents together sought orders for restraints on other parties from dealing with the jewellery held by the seventh respondent, restraints on other parties from causing payment to be made to the wife prior to the payments allegedly owed to the seventh and fifteenth respondents, a declaration for a lien over assets or property held or entitled to the wife, and costs. In the alternative, they sought a lump sum payment of $855,573.00. They also sought orders relating to the dealing with the jewellery and related proceeds, and ancillary costs.

AF Pty Ltd (the eighth respondent)

The eighth respondent sought a suite of orders, contained within their Amended Minute of Order dated 4 June 2025. These orders included a declaration that the eighth respondent is entitled to a lien over assets or property of the wife, for the amount of professional costs and disbursements allegedly owing, a declaration that this lien has priority ranking above the other lawyers’ claims, a declaration that the professional costs and disbursements allegedly owing were secured by an equitable charge over the Suburb J property, a declaration that the fourteenth respondent was on notice of the eighth respondent’s security over the Suburb J property, restraints upon parties from causing payment or transfer of assets or property to the wife prior to payment to the eighth respondent, an enforcement of Order 6 of the orders made on 7 July 2023. Orders were also sought for costs.
At hearing of this matter, the question of relief being sought in the form of a mortgage in respect of the jewellery was raised. Ultimately, the eighth respondent was not permitted to pursue that ground of relief, where it was not sought in a timely fashion.

Mr AH (the ninth respondent)

The ninth respondent, in his Outline of Case document filed 12 March 2025, sought orders to secure a (purported) $2 million loan repayment plus interest, a declaration that he is entitled to recover this amount from the sale of the Suburb G property, and dismissal of various claims sought by the wife (including relief sought against the ninth respondent). He also sought costs.

AK Pty Ltd (the twelfth respondent)

The twelfth respondent filed a submitting notice on 28 February 2025 and sought to submit to any order the Court may make in the proceeding. It did not want to be heard on the question of costs.

ZZ Pty Ltd (the fourteenth respondent)

The fourteenth respondent sought to rely upon the orders sought in its amended application, which was purportedly filed on 7 March 2025. For reasons unknown at the time of the hearing, the application was rejected for filing, although it appeared to have been served upon all parties. To remedy this, it was marked as Exhibit R14-6 in the proceedings.
The orders sought were for a declaration of entitlement to a lien over the assets and/or property of the applicant wife up to the amount of $466,168.95 plus interest allegedly owing, an order restraining other parties from causing payment or transfer of asset or property to the wife prior to payment of the amount allegedly owing to the fourteenth respondent, that various orders sought by the applicant wife be dismissed, a declaration that the debt owing is secured by a charge over the Suburb J property, orders relating to the sale of the Suburb J property and related mortgage, various ancillary orders and costs.

HISTORY OF THE RELATIONSHIP

The applicant wife, Ms Pirani, born in 1987, and respondent husband, Mr Pirani, born in 1968, commenced a relationship in 2007, married in 2013 and separated in mid-February 2023. There are three children of the marriage, X, born in 2013, Y, born in 2015 and Z, born in 2018 (“the children”). When together the parties lived a lavish, expensive lifestyle, exemplified by the husband’s purchase of luxury Motor Vehicle 1 for the wife in 2019.
In early 2009, B Pty Ltd, a property development company, was formed by the husband. The wife says she commenced working at B Pty Ltd when it started as a secretary but received no income, however the husband paid for her expenses and college or university fees.
In 2012 the husband purchased a property in Suburb PP (the wife says for her[2]) and they commenced living together in approximately 2012.
From about 2012 to 2013 the wife commenced receiving income from her employment at B Pty Ltd.
The parties married in Australia in 2013. The wife says the marriage was not registered in Country P. Although Ms OO had been requested to register the marriage in Country P, the husband discontinued the process.[3] This was in a context where the husband had previously been married and entered into a property settlement with his previous wife in Australia. However, it was further contended that his marriage to his previous wife has not been dissolved in Country P.
In 2016 the property at F Street, Suburb G in New South Wales (“the [Suburb G] property”) was purchased in the husband’s sole name. In 2016 the existing home was demolished, and a new home was constructed. The wife describes heavy involvement in the building of the new home.
The wife says that by about 2017 she was receiving income of about $10,000 per month, being monies that she applied for the wellbeing of the family, including for the purchase of furniture and household items. The wife describes that she also attended to the cooking and cleaning of the home when not working.
The parties moved into the Suburb G home in 2018.
The children were attending at KK School Suburb BB and LL School up until the commencement of proceedings.
The parties purchased a home for the wife’s parents to live in, in early 2020 in Suburb J (“the [Suburb J] property”).[4]
The parties travelled to Country P in late 2022, with the wife and the children returning to Australia in early 2023 so the children could commence the new school year. The husband remained in Country P. The children have remained in the wife’s sole care since then.
The parties separated shortly after the wife’s return to Australia. The wife commenced proceedings in June 2023, then obtaining urgent ex parte orders against the husband.
The wife describes that since separation the husband has ceased to pay for the children’s school fees, resulting in them being withdrawn from their previous schools and enrolled in another school.
In February 2023 the family home was raided by the NSW Police who seized a sum of money that has since been forfeited to the NSW Crime Commission.
The wife’s income ceased in about April 2023, and around this time her access to the husband’s debit card ceased. Shortly prior to her access to the card ending, she withdrew $50,000 from that account.
In about April 2023 the wife’s luxury Motor Vehicle 1 was repossessed by B Pty Ltd from a shopping centre while she and the children were inside the shopping centre, eating. In May 2023 the wife was provided with another vehicle.
Similarly, some time after separation, in mid-2024, the husband caused the wife to be locked out of the former family home, evicting her through the use of security staff. In addition to the above, the wife contends that she has been subjected to other family violence, in the form of threats, coercion and physical violence, in causing the wife to be followed by private investigators, and in the form of financial coercion following separation. Little, if any, attention was given to these matters at trial, and not sufficient to determine that the wife’s contributions were rendered significantly more arduous.
The husband was permitted to participate in these proceedings by video link. He shows no sign of ever returning to Australia following the demise of his business interests as illustrated by the involvement of liquidators in these proceedings. As a part of the liquidation process, the liquidators have identified substantial claims on the husband’s business interests and estimate a shortfall of not less than $300,000,000.[5]

PRINCIPLES

The final hearing in this matter started prior to the commencement of the Family Law Amendment Act 2024 (Cth).
The foundation for the exercise of the powers to adjust the property interests of the parties pursuant to s 79 of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”) is that it must be just and equitable, both to adjust the property interests at all (Stanford v Stanford (2012) 247 CLR 108)[6] and to adjust them in a particular manner (Bevan v Bevan (2013) 49 Fam LR 387).[7]
The necessary first step in consideration of whether it is just and equitable to make an order is to identify “according to ordinary common law and equitable principles the existing legal and equitable interests of the parties in the property”.[8]
This first step constitutes the primary area of complexity and controversy in this case, in particular in determining the manner in which the parties’ interests in the real property and the jewellery are the subject of interests held by the various third parties.
Section 79 further requires that the contributions of the parties are to be given consideration.
In Dickons & Dickons [2012] FamCAFC 154; [2012] 50 Fam LR 244 the Full Court said that:[9]
the requirements of the section are met by approaching the assessment of contributions holistically and by analysing the nature, form, characteristics and origin of the property currently comprising that to which s 79 applies, and, in turn, analysing the nature, form and extent of the contributions (of all types) contemplated by s 79). That task is also undertaken by reference to the nature and form of the particular marriage partnership manifested by the particular circumstances of this particular marriage. Is it, for example, a relationship, as Deane J put it in Mallett at 640-641 ‘...where the parties have adopted the attitude that their marriage constituted a practical union of both lives and property...” or is it, for example, a union where parties lived very separate domestic and financial lives?
This requires that “[a]ny and all such contributions, whether or not they sound in, or are directly linked to, the property available for distribution, should be considered and assessed together with the nature, form and extent of all other contributions of all types contemplated otherwise by s 79(4)”.[10]
As part of that consideration:[11]
s 79(4) specifically requires the Court to take into account contributions made to the welfare of the family (and substantively and “...not in any merely token way...”; see, Mallett v Mallett [1984] HCA 21; (1984) 156 CLR 605 at 636 per Wilson J) notwithstanding that those contributions may not be, or cannot be seen to be, directly linked to the available property at trial, or any increase or decrease in the value of the property.
Again, in Dickons it was noted that the assessment of contributions did not necessitate the identification of a causal link between the contribution and the property but, adopting In the Marriage of Aleksovski [1996] FamCAFC 111; (1996) FLC 92-705 (“Alekovski”)[12], described that:[13]
the s 79 discretion involves as a necessary requirement that “... trial Judges weigh and assess the contributions of all kinds and from all sources made by each of the parties throughout the period of their cohabitation and then translate such an assessment into a percentage of the overall property of the parties or provide for a transfer of property in specie in accordance with that assessment.”
In Aleksovski, Kay J explained that “[w]hat is important is to somehow give a reasonable value to all of the elements that go to making up the entirety of the marriage relationship”.[14]
In this case it appears that the husband’s focus was largely upon his previously extensive property development interests. The wife, who had worked for him held, it appears, primary responsibility in respect of the care of the children. Both undertook such with professional support as may be expected to have been available to them in accordance with their apparent wealth. The wife further took responsibility for coordinating their combined charitable efforts.
A significant aspect of this case is that the parties have together ridden the highs, and now separately, the lows of the property development exploits of the husband. For a period they together enjoyed an lavish lifestyle, featuring a luxury home, significant expenditure on consumables and personal interests, private schooling for children and perhaps best exemplified by the husband’s notorious gifting of an exotic car to the wife (although not, as it turns out, a true gifting, as the vehicle was later repossessed by his company).
As will become apparent, what primarily remains is liability that can never be met, the publicly displayed wealth of the parties being a hollow artifice.
As well as the established liabilities as set out below, it will become apparent that there are also contingent liabilities. Of greatest significance are the contingent liabilities of the wife in respect of extensive legal fees.
While it will be necessary, to the extent that the evidence allows it, to have regard to the practical impact of the wife’s legal liabilities on her actual circumstances under any order made in these proceedings, as a general proposition, as described by the Full Court in NHC v RCH [2004] FamCA 633 at [55], the liability a party has for the payment of legal fees is not a matter to be taken into account in ascertaining the pool of property. As observed by Kay J in In the Marriage of Farnell [1995] FamCA 140; (1995) 128 FLR 374, to take such a matter into account in determining the pool of property places a costs burden upon the other party in a manner that sits ill with the approach to costs set out a s 117 (now s 114UB) of the Act.
The court is further to give consideration to the matters set out at s 75(2) of the Family Law Act. In this case, the ongoing care for the children that will be undertaken by the wife, with little if any prospect of support from the father is such a matter. Not only may it be anticipated that the wife will carry the practical burden of that care for a significant period of time, but the prospect of the wife receiving financial support from the husband appears dubious at best, as illustrated by the children’s removal from private schooling following the husband’s absenting of himself from Australia to Country P.

THE ISSUES IN THIS CASE

The dominant issue in this case is establishing the content of the pool of the property of the parties and the interests that those assets are subject to. There are various creditors’ claims, mortgages and other securities against the property.
The key items of property can be identified as follows:
(a) Proceeds of the sale of the Suburb G property;

(b) B Group;

(c) D1 Pty Ltd;

(d) QQ Street, Suburb J;

(e) Jewellery held by Ms OO; and

(f) The husband’s properties in Country P.

Although the wife referred to the husband’s superannuation by her case outline document, no evidence was offered as to the existence of any superannuation interest.
A considerable focus of the trial was as to debts owed by the husband and wife, and their impact upon the property identified above.

Suburb G property

The first key item of property was the parties’ former home (or rather, the proceeds from its sale) in Suburb G, New South Wales, being a home held in the husband’s name. That home was subject to a first mortgage held in favour of Westpac Banking which all parties recognise as the pre-eminent claim upon that property. Secondly, the ninth respondent Mr AH secured a loan to the husband in the sum of $2.8 million by way of a mortgage against that property. Although this was contentious at the commencement of the trial, during the trial the relevant parties entered into consent orders recognising Mr AH’s interest in the sum of $2.8 million as following that of Westpac Banking Corporations, but preceding any other creditors in relation to the Suburb G property. The third claim against that property comes from B Pty Ltd who asserts that various directors loans made to the husband by B Pty Ltd are secured by mortgage against the Suburb G property. This is a claim that is formally recognised by the wife but disputed by the husband in circumstances which will be set out further below.
The balance of the proceeds, after the payment of the Westpac and Mr AH mortgages, is (uncontentiously) approximately $3.9 million.[15]

QQ Street – The Suburb J property

The next asset of significance is an item of real estate held at Suburb J, New South Wales, registered in the wife’s name. The wife asserts that she holds the property on trust for her mother, who occupies the property. That proposition was the subject of direct challenge.
The wife describes that the property was purchased for her parents in early 2020, but that her father has since passed away.
The wife says that there were discussions between herself, the husband and her mother, where her mother asked for help to have her own home, saying that she only had $40,000 as a deposit. By her affidavit, the wife says that she understands that although the husband did not want to take money from her mother, he did, at her insistence. How this came to be understood remained unclear. She says that the husband told her mother that “[t]his house will always be for you and you don’t have to pay anything for it”.[16] The wife says that the husband gave her the $40,000 in cash, which was later seized as described below.
She asserts that her mother would often save $2,000 to $5,000 and give it to the husband, adding up to a further $30,000 that was also held in the home and seized. The wife did not explain how she came to know of the asserted transactions with the husband. The wife did not call her mother to give any evidence about the Suburb J property. The wife described that she retained monies from her mother in a particular luxury bag that was seized by the NSW police. She says that she told the Crimes Commission both that the money was for her mother, and also that it was her mother’s way of paying for the deposit, toward her mother’s promise to keep paying money toward the property. Ultimately this money formed part of that which was forfeited to the Commission.
That property is subject to a registered mortgage held by D1 Pty Ltd, who filed a submitting appearance. In her affidavit filed 13 March 2025 the wife said that “[D1 Pty Ltd] holds a mortgage over the property at [QQ Street], [Suburb J]”.[17]
The wife, during the trial of the matter, sought for the first time to contest that mortgage. Given the late stage at which she identified this challenge, and that she had not placed D1 Pty Ltd on notice of the change in relief that she sought in relation to which they had filed their submitting notice, she was not permitted to mount such a challenge.
Any debt associated with that mortgage has not been identified or proven. At best the mortgage document acknowledges, at Exhibit A2, a sum of $875,000 or such other sum agreed upon by the Mortgagor and the Mortgagee. What the actual sum might be remained a mystery. Significant confusion emerged from the document as on its face it indicated that the sum was owed by D1 Pty Ltd to the wife rather than the other way around. No evidence was adduced as to the actual sum, beyond an assertion made by the husband on reading the mortgage document. There is no good reason to place any credence upon assertions made by the husband.
The husband further asserted that monies given to the wife would be rent, generally denying the wife’s account. He further asserted that he conducted renovations on the property, to create a granny flat. He asserted that he had paid the rates on the property until “the day I flee to [Country P]” subsequently asserting this meant “fly”. Again, no credence can be placed on the husband’s assertions.
The contentious issue then was whether the property is merely held on trust by the wife for her mother.
Unlike her Amended Amended Initiating Application, in her outline of case document the wife did not pursue this claim. By her proposed balance sheet the wife conceded a value of $1 million. By her case outline document the wife did not dispute the presence of a mortgage, but alleged it was entered into as a transaction to defeat her claim, a matter not pursued at trial.
In final submissions the wife asserted that $70,000 of the $250,000 taken by the NSW Crimes Commission was her money, paid to her by her parents as a repayment of a loan for the Suburb J property, further asserting that she holds the property on constructive trust for her parents.
In the submissions the contention was that the wife’s mother paid between $70,000 and $90,000 in ongoing payments, where the purchase price was $800,000.
I am unable to accept the wife’s account that she holds the property on trust for her parents. It is a claim made inconsistently by her. It is a claim made on sparse evidence, and wholly reliant even to that degree upon the wife. Despite the wife’s assertion that there are others who could support the account, including her mother and other relatives, she called none to support her contention. It can only be inferred that they would not have assisted the wife’s case in this respect. While some money appears to have been paid to the wife, in a sum of perhaps $70,000 by the wife’s mother, I remain unclear as to on what basis this may have occurred.
The Suburb J property, registered in the wife’s name, is one in relation to which the wife holds the beneficial interest.

Seized cash

The third item of property was a sum of $250,000 cash seized by the New South Wales police force from the Suburb G property in 2023 and, as at the commencement of the trial, the subject of a notice served by the New South Wales Crimes Commission. Although the wife initially sought that this be declared to be her monies, by the completion of the trial the wife and husband adopted the common position that the monies were now forfeited (the wife having apparently received a notice to that effect) and should no longer be considered to be a part of the pool of property.

Jewellery

The next item of property is jewellery currently held in AB Pty Ltd by Ms OO, being jewellery purchased for the wife by the husband during the relationship and accordingly, subject to any other claims, jewellery owned by the wife. The wife estimates that the total value of the jewellery is approximately $702,000[18] (although in her Case Outline Document she asserted a worth of $800,000), identifying a number of specific items and assigning a specific value to each. At its best this constitutes, in the event that the wife is to retain the jewellery, an admission against interest.
Although there was a claim by the husband that the wife has other jewellery, this claim was not sufficiently supported to result in a finding of such jewellery.

Before moving further through the evidence it is appropriate to make some observations about the husband’s evidence. Neither the limited affidavit evidence filed by him, nor the manner and content of his oral evidence gave any confidence that he could be relied upon, at all, in the absence of supportive evidence. He failed to provide adequate disclosure, at times asserting that various matters, such as his holdings in Country P, were not the business of the Court. In his affidavit, describing the lack of value of the properties, he asserted that he was “happy to transfer half of the properties in [Country P]” to the wife.[19] He did not specify how the half would be determined, it perhaps being of little significance given his description of their collective value. In his oral evidence he asserted that only some had come from his father, and displayed that his assertion in his affidavit that he was “happy” to transfer half to the wife was disingenuous, his contention being that the Country P properties have nothing to do with this court.

B Pty Ltd

The second respondent, B Pty Ltd, was one of approximately 80 companies forming a part of the B Group, controlled by the husband as director and owned by the husband, either directly or indirectly, as the shareholder. Those corporate entities are each the subject of administration.
In general terms, the B Group was involved in the purchase, sale and development of real estate. Corporate entities were created for each project, and finances obtained by loans guaranteed and secured by the other corporate entities within the group.
B Pty Ltd is significant in these proceedings, both as the central component of the matrimonial property and, in liquidation, as a creditor of the husband with mortgage security over the Suburb G property.
The debts asserted as owed by B Pty Ltd would result in B Pty Ltd being of no residual value in these proceedings.
The debt claimed by B Pty Ltd against the Suburb G proceeds would result in that property having no residual value in these proceedings.
The circumstances of the B Group and subsequent appointment of administrators are set out in the affidavit of Ms AY. Mr BF and Ms AY were appointed as administrators of B Pty Ltd, D2 Pty Ltd and E Pty Ltd (the second, fifth and sixth respondents to these proceedings respectively) on 7 July 2023. On 14 July 2023, they were appointed administrators of 65 further related companies.
On 14 July 2023 the balance of B Group, being a further seven companies, were placed in liquidation, albeit separately administered.
On 16 April 2024, 55 of the entities administered by Ms AY and Mr BF executed deeds of company arrangement. A further two entities entered into liquidation.
On 25 February 2025, they were then appointed liquidators of B Group (excluding those otherwise administered, and excluding eight companies in the group that had not yet entered liquidation but were subject to deeds of company arrangement pending liquidation). It is in liquidation that B Pty Ltd seeks relief in these proceedings.
The shortfall in meeting the debts of B Group is anticipated to exceed $300 million. The liquidators do not anticipate any significant return to unsecured creditors. By his affidavit the husband conceded that the loans in respect of B Pty Ltd exceeded the assets of B Pty Ltd.
Although the three entities, B Pty Ltd, D2 Pty Ltd and E Pty Ltd were all joined to the proceedings, relief was ultimately only pursued by B Pty Ltd, and ultimately only in relation to the husband’s contest as to the quantum of debt owed by him to B Pty Ltd and secured against the Suburb G property.
Ms AY identified that as part of the liquidation process, the accounts of B Pty Ltd were reconciled to the end of 2023. This was described as a necessary step to identify the dealings within B Group. As part of that process the loan account maintained by B Pty Ltd in respect of the husband was also brought up to date. The liquidators had access to the accounting system, computer server, emails and hard copy documents maintained by B Pty Ltd.
It was the indebtedness in relation to this loan account that was the subject of contest in the proceedings, the loan account being secured by a mortgage held by B Pty Ltd over the Suburb G property. The wife did not ultimately contest B Pty Ltd’s claim in relation to the Suburb G property.
The first relevant loan agreement was entered into by the husband and B Pty Ltd on 28 April 2023 and recorded the then principal amount of $2,308,798. It provided for further borrowings to take place during the term of the agreement to be deemed as loaned pursuant to the agreement. An interest rate of ten per cent was stipulated, and the loans were then secured by a charge over the Suburb G property.
On 10 May 2023, several months prior to the appointment of the administrators, the husband and B Pty Ltd varied the agreement to increase the principal sum to $3,255,583.
Although the liquidators identified that at various times the husband has alleged that the loan agreements were fraudulent, in the face of detailed evidence supporting the proposition that the husband entered into the agreements, the husband mounted no coherent challenge to the veracity of the agreement documents.
B Pty Ltd placed a caveat on the Suburb G title on 29 April 2023 in reliance upon the charge created by the loan document. On 10 May 2023 a mortgage was placed on the title per the May 2023 agreement.
It should be observed that, by his trial affidavit the husband made no effective contest of any of these matters. He adduced no evidence to establish that the loan amounts asserted by the liquidator were incorrect. He mounted no effective challenge to the evidence of the liquidator, including as to the liquidators declining to account the loans as reduced by dividends or by payments from the husband’s salary due to lack of substantiation as set out later. He, however, complained during the proceedings that the amount was not correct.
The liquidator described that, prior to their appointment the husband took monies from the B Pty Ltd bank account for personal use, with such recorded on the books of B Pty Ltd as a loan account.
As at the appointment of the liquidators on 7 July 2023 the B Pty Ltd accounts recorded an outstanding debt of $6,599,707.18. The ledgers, which were produced, support the existence of outstanding director’s loans in that sum. However, Ms AY explains that analysis by comparison of the ledgers to the books and records of B Pty Ltd show total director’s borrowings by the husband in the sum of $7,994,381.27.
This included significant payments of an ostensibly personal nature to the husband, including criminal lawyer’s fees for himself and his daughter, school fees, tax for his daughter, tax for himself, deposit for the Suburb G property, and mortgage payments toward the Suburb G property. At the end of the financial year, the company would declare (by recording entries) dividends paid to the husband which would, if genuine, reduce the amount he owed to B Pty Ltd with respect to the loan account.
The liquidator undertook a reconciliation in respect of the borrowings that included borrowings from related entities. A number of entries (which would have otherwise served to reduce the debt owed by the husband) were identified by the liquidators as excluded by them in reaching a conclusion as to the indebtedness. Of greatest significance was the exclusion of purported payments of dividends allocated to the husband to the loan account, and the crediting against the loan account of salary allocations. This last aspect appeared to be the primary focus of the husband’s complaints as to the calculation of the indebtedness.
The liquidator identifies that the loan account ledgers recorded that from 1 January 2020, a total of $4,414,914.67 was credited against the loan accounts in favour of the husband and described as dividend distributions.[20] The liquidator has not reckoned this amount to reduce the loan accounts of the husband on the basis that no profit was earned in the relevant years to enable a dividend to be paid. That is, the crediting of those amounts as a dividend is fundamentally inconsistent with the company accounts.
The loan ledgers were conceded to record credits in the form of salary allocations to the husband, totalling $575,048.55. These, too, were not taken by the liquidator to reduce the indebtedness on the basis that there was a lack of information to substantiate the payments.
As noted above these appeared to be the focus of the husband’s claims that he did not owe monies to B Pty Ltd. In support of this claim he referred in his submissions to tax payments made. The liquidator identified that a number of tax payments were included on the loan ledger for both the husband and his daughter from his previous marriage. It remained unclear how the payment of tax by the husband, the payment of which resulted in increased loans to meet the tax liability, established that salary or dividends had in reality been paid to reduce the debt. Perhaps what was being suggested was that the payment of tax established the veracity of the claims. However, at least in relation to the purported dividends, such did not counter the proposition raised by the liquidators that there were no profits available to be distributed, and the payment of the tax did not establish otherwise.
While the payment of taxes could support the proposition that salaries were paid, no adequate substantiation was made by the husband to establish that the loans were so reduced.
Similarly, debits recorded in the loan ledgers in relation to fringe benefit taxes in the sum of $983,061.25 were not taken into account as they were lacking substantiation.
Again, on the basis of a lack of supporting information, a purported debt in relation to the Suburb J property was not included.
However, various substantiated credits to the loan account were applied to reduce the loan amount.
The reconciliation also captured payments made by related entities to the benefit of the husband. These were included in the indebtedness to B Pty Ltd as they formed loans from the related entity to B Pty Ltd, then loans to the husband within the accounts of B Pty Ltd and, accordingly, loans within the scope of the loan agreement between the husband and B Pty Ltd, being loan amounts secured by mortgage against the Suburb G property.
The reconciliation of the loan account, as set out above, then led to a total greater than that initially seen in the accounts, of $7,994,381.27.
Written submissions for the second, fifth and sixth respondents were provided to the Court, dated 20 March 2025. These submissions were exhibited as Exhibit R2-4. The effect of these submissions was to provide a final quantum for the loan between B Pty Ltd and the husband, which amounted to $11,211,703 inclusive of interest as provided for by the loan agreement entered into by the husband with B Pty Ltd. However, at the hearing of this matter, that amount was reduced to $9,590,724, on the basis explained below.
The difference between the two amounts provided by B Pty Ltd was the result of a concession as to the date from which interest should be calculated. It is contained within the schedule annexed to the written submissions. The larger amount was calculated by adding interest to debts from when they were incurred. Rather, the smaller number (which B Pty Ltd sought to rely upon at the hearing) calculates interest only from when the husband acknowledged it was payable in the loan agreement until 20 March 2025.
In the context of the failure of the husband to mount any meaningful or substantive challenge to it, the evidence of the liquidator as to the indebtedness of the husband to B Pty Ltd, as secured against the Suburb G proceeds should be accepted.
The effect of this conclusion is that Suburb G no longer constitutes a substantive asset for distribution between the parties, the remaining balance being insufficient to meet the secured indebtedness of $9,590,724.

D1 Pty Ltd

The husband is the sole shareholder and director of D1 Pty Ltd, part of B Group. The entity is currently under external administration with receivers and managers appointed. Its sole asset is the mortgage in relation to the Suburb J property. Exhibit A2 is a Mortgage Form lodged with the Office of the Registrar General purportedly executed on 3 April 2020 (but dated 9 April 2020) for a principal sum of $875,000 or another sum as agreed over land title reference [...] (the Suburb J property). As identified above, there was insufficient evidence to establish any quantum of indebtedness associated with this mortgage.

Husband’s superannuation interests

In his financial statement filed 29 June 2023 the husband deposed to $0 worth of superannuation assets. The husband did not discuss any superannuation interests in his affidavit or other evidence. In her Outline of Case Document attempted to file 18 March 2025, the wife lists two super funds in which she suggests the husband holds balances totalling approximately $54,039. There was no evidence adduced to support these assertions.

THE SOLICITORS’ CLAIMS

The three sets of solicitors make claims that the wife owes legal costs to them, and as to corresponding rights of enforcement attaching to the property interests to be held by the wife.
To secure these amounts AF Pty Ltd (the eight respondent) and ZZ Pty Ltd (fourteenth respondent) rely both upon a charge purportedly created by the costs agreement entered into by the wife and then upon a claim of a fruits of litigation lien. Ms OO (seventh and fifteenth respondent) relies upon a possessory lien and a fruits of litigation lien as security for her claim.
They claim that pursuant to a costs agreement the wife owes a sum in accordance with the invoices presented by each practice.
As to AF Pty Ltd and ZZ Pty Ltd, although the wife accepts that there are monies owing, the wife does not accept the asserted sums as representing the proper amounts. As to Ms OO, the wife disputes any amount owing and, in particular, whether Ms OO was retained as the wife’s legal representative.
Each of the claims made by the solicitors relies then at first upon the validity of the costs agreements.

The costs agreements

Each practice relies upon a purported Costs Agreement that, pursuant to s 184 of the Legal Profession Uniform Law 2014 NSW (“the Uniform Law”) may, subject to that law, be enforceable as any other contract.
The costs agreements are governed by Division 4 of Part 4.3 of the Uniform Law, which provides that the agreement must be written, or evidenced in writing, and that it may be accepted by conduct. Section 184 of the Uniform Law provides that:
Subject to this Law, a costs agreement may be enforced in the same way as any other contract.
The foundational provision in relation to legal costs is at s 172 which emphasises that costs must be proportionate, reasonably incurred, and reasonable in amount. It further sets out the role of a costs agreement in determining whether the costs are reasonable. It is in the following terms:
172 Legal costs must be fair and reasonable
(1) A law practice must, in charging legal costs, charge costs that are no more than fair and reasonable in all the circumstances and that in particular are—
(a) proportionately and reasonably incurred; and

(b) proportionate and reasonable in amount.
(2) In considering whether legal costs satisfy subsection (1), regard must be had to whether the legal costs reasonably reflect—
(a) the level of skill, experience, specialisation and seniority of the lawyers concerned; and

(b) the level of complexity, novelty or difficulty of the issues involved, and the extent to which the matter involved a matter of public interest; and

(c) the labour and responsibility involved; and

(d) the circumstances in acting on the matter, including (for example) any or all of the following—

(i) the urgency of the matter;

(ii) the time spent on the matter;

(iii) the time when business was transacted in the matter;

(iv) the place where business was transacted in the matter;

(v) the number and importance of any documents involved; and

(e) the quality of the work done; and
(f) the retainer and the instructions (express or implied) given in the matter.
(3) In considering whether legal costs are fair and reasonable, regard must also be had to whether the legal costs conform to any applicable requirements of this Part, the Uniform Rules and any fixed costs legislative provisions.

(4) A costs agreement is prima facie evidence that legal costs disclosed in the agreement are fair and reasonable if—

(a) the provisions of Division 3 relating to costs disclosure have been complied with; and

(b) the costs agreement does not contravene, and was not entered into in contravention of, any provision of Division 4.

In addition to being proportionate, s 174 requires prompt disclosure as to the basis for costs to be calculated and an estimate of total legal costs. The estimate forms part of an ongoing obligation of the legal practice to update advice where there are significant changes, including as to the expected total costs. Section 174 further requires disclosure of other matters including as to dispute resolution and imposes a requirement on the practice to “take all reasonable steps to satisfy itself that the client has understood and given consent to he proposed course of action for the conduct of the matter and the proposed costs”.
Section 178 provides that contravention of the disclosure obligations renders a costs agreement void, defers liability to pay costs until they are assessed, and prohibits the commencement or maintenance of proceedings to recover costs until the assessment or dispute has been resolved by the local authority. Further the contravention may constitute unsatisfactory professional conduct or professional misconduct.
Those consequences are subject to modification pursuant to the Legal Profession Uniform General Rules 2015 (“the LPUGR”). The LPUGR relevantly provide that:
72A Non-compliance with disclosure obligations—disapplication of section 178(1) and (2) of the Uniform Law

(1) This rule applies where a law practice has contravened the disclosure obligations of Part 4.3 of the Uniform Law in relation to a particular matter.

(2) Section 178(1) and (2) of the Uniform Law do not apply in relation to the law practice (so far as they would otherwise apply to the matter concerned) in circumstances where the relevant authority, a costs assessor, a court or a tribunal is satisfied that—
(a) the law practice took reasonable steps to comply with the disclosure obligations of Part 4.3 of the Uniform Law before becoming aware of the contravention, and

(b) the law practice, no later than 14 days after the date on which it became aware of the contravention, rectified the contravention, as far as practicable, by providing the client with the necessary information required to be disclosed under Division 3 of Part 4.3 of the Uniform Law (including, where relevant, an estimate or revised estimate of the costs), and

(c) the contravention was not substantial and it would not be reasonable to expect that the client would have made a different decision in any relevant respect.
(3) Subrule (2)(b) applies even though the information or estimate is not provided at the times required by the disclosure obligations of Part 4.3 of the Uniform Law.
Division 5 of Part 4.3 of the Uniform Law deals with billing. Section 192 provides that:
192 Notification of client’s rights

A law practice must ensure that a bill includes or is accompanied by a written statement setting out—
(a) the avenues that are open to the client in the event of a dispute in relation to legal costs; and

(b) any time limits that apply to the taking of any action referred to in paragraph (a).

Section 194 places a restriction on commencing proceedings to cover legal costs, in circumstances where the requirements of s 192 (and other sections of the Uniform Law and Rules) have not been complied with.
Section 194 is in the following terms:
194 Restriction on commencing proceedings to recover legal costs
(1) A law practice must not commence legal proceedings to recover legal costs from a person unless a bill has been given for the legal costs and the bill complies with the requirements of this Law and the Uniform Rules.

(2) A law practice must not commence legal proceedings to recover legal costs from a person who has been given a bill until—

(a) where the legal costs are the subject of a costs dispute before the designated local regulatory authority—the authority has closed or resolved the dispute; and

(b) at least 30 days after the later of—

(i) the date on which the person is given the bill; or

(ii) the date on which the person receives an itemised bill following a request made in accordance with section 187.

If any of the bills of costs are found to be non-compliant with s 192, the issue then arises as to the effect that s 194 has on the proceedings. As observed by the NSW Supreme Court in Frontier Law Group Pty Ltd v Barkman [2016] NSWSC 1542 at [39], “it seems impossible for the solicitor to be able to establish a right to commence these legal proceedings, unless a memorandum of costs is tendered, which, on its face, complies with the law and the Uniform Law”. This was considered in the Local Court case of Hodges v Sathyanparamatheva [2019] NSWLC 8, where a failure to go further than merely stating that avenues to dispute a bill of costs existed was held in non-compliance with s 192. As a result, the proceedings to recover costs in that matter was dismissed. Rule 72A, which provides exceptions for other breaches of the Uniform Law, does not apply to such a case.

AF Pty Ltd

AF Pty Ltd sought declaratory relief as to their security over the Suburb J property. AF Pty Ltd sought no quantification of the debt associated with the security.
The affidavit of Mr BA identifies that the wife first attended upon him on 24 April 2023, in the company of Ms OO. Mr BA asserts that during this meeting he provided an estimate of costs and disbursements, of approximately $1 million, if not more, to final hearing. A further estimate of $1.5 million, excluding disbursements was given at that meeting by Special Counsel, Ms BG. Instructions were then given by the wife to commence proceedings on an urgent basis in relation to both property and parenting. The wife was asked to deposit $10,000 into the AF Pty Ltd trust account, which was done on 26 April 2023. On that day a fee disclosure from Mr BH SC was provided to the wife. Further instructions were taken from the wife the following day, and circumstances of urgency were identified.
On 6 May 2023 a draft court document was provided to the wife, along with advice that the provision of a Costs Agreement was delayed due to the urgency of instructions, and a further estimate was given, amending the earlier estimate, replacing it with the sum of $3.5 million.
On or about 8 May 2023 the wife was provided with a fee disclosure document from Mr BJ of counsel.
On 10 May 2023 the wife was provided with a costs agreement from AF Pty Ltd. The agreement was explained, along with the option to obtain independent legal advice. The wife executed the document.
Further work was undertaken, and interim orders were obtained on 7 June 2023, and further relief on 7 July 2023.
A further costs agreement was provided to the wife on 5 August 2023, which was executed by the wife on 15 August 2023.
On 29 August 2023 the wife instructed AF Pty Ltd to suspend work, and then terminated the retainer on or about 7 September 2023.
On 20 September 2023 AF Pty Ltd served on ZZ Pty Ltd (who were the wife’s subsequent lawyers) notice in respect of a claimed lien, and a provided proposed deed to secure outstanding fees.
On 13 September 2024 AF Pty Ltd applied to join these proceedings.
Seven tax invoices were issued by AF Pty Ltd to the wife between 30 June 2023 and 13 October 2023, totalling $854,111.15.

The wife’s specific complaint regarding AF Pty Ltd

The wife resisted AF Pty Ltd’s claim on the basis of failure to comply with obligations imposed by s 174, which deals with prompt disclosure of the basis of charging, an estimate of fees, and an obligation to take all reasonable steps to ensure that the client has understood and given consent to the proposed course of action and the proposed costs.
Having initially consulted them on or about 24 April 2023, a costs agreement was not provided for another two weeks. The wife asserts that this did not meet the requirement of the Uniform Law that a costs agreement be provided as soon as practicable. As a result, she contends that the agreement is void and an assessment of costs would be required to be pursued potentially in another jurisdiction.

Discussion of AF Pty Ltd’s claim

AF Pty Ltd asserts that the estimates and fee agreement were provided as soon as practicable in accordance with s 174 of the Uniform Law. At the first consultation estimates were provided, then updated with the provision of a costs agreement about two weeks after initial instructions had been taken. AF Pty Ltd contends that this meets the requirement.
The explanation for the delay was the undertaking of urgent work. By the description contained in the affidavit of Mr BA it can be concluded that the wife was facing circumstances that required urgent and complex work both in and out of regular business hours.
It can be seen that estimates were promptly provided, and then promptly updated, that identified the high level of anticipated expense associated with the case.
The key issue is the provision of the costs agreement that identified the basis of the charging, the ability to negotiate the terms, along with information regarding itemisation, bills and disputes.
What is required is that such be provided as soon as practicable. What is also required by s 174(3) of the Uniform Law is the obligation to be satisfied of a client’s understanding. This is an essential aspect of the scheme set out at s 174. What is required are circumstances that permit informed consent on the part of the client. This shapes the understanding of “practicable” at s 174(1).
The circumstances of each case will determine what is “as soon as practicable after instructions are initially given.” Here the urgency and intensity of the work being undertaken is proffered as circumstances rendering a gap of 16 days between the taking of initial instructions and the provision of the agreement. During that time, it appears, much work was undertaken and significant legal costs incurred by the wife. Two barristers were consulted who promptly provided fee disclosure documents that were provided to the wife.
The urgency and intensity of the work perhaps explain why attention was not given immediately to the costs agreements. It does not establish, however, a lack of practicability in the period prior to the provision of the agreement.
Although prompt estimates were provided, it should not be concluded that, as soon as practicable after instructions were initially given, AF Pty Ltd provided the wife with information disclosing the basis on which legal costs would be calculated, or the associated necessary information as set out at s 174(2), including as to the ability to negotiate a costs agreement, to negotiate the billing method, or to dispute costs via a regulatory agency.
This renders AF Pty Ltd in breach of the s 174 obligations.
The consequence is that, pursuant to s 178, the costs agreement is void and there is no requirement to pay legal costs until assessment or resolution of a costs dispute by the local authority. Further, AF Pty Ltd is restrained from commencing or maintaining proceedings for the recovery of costs. This result is, however, subject to the operation of Rule 72A of the LPUGR, which is set out above.
As identified above, Rule 72A outlines that in certain circumstances a failure to meet the requirements Part 4.3 of the Uniform Law as it relates to costs disclosure does not attract the serious consequences imposed by s 178.
There are three conjunctive limbs to Rule 72A. They are the requirement to have taken reasonable steps to comply before becoming aware of the contravention, correction within 14 days of becoming aware of the contravention, and that the contravention was not substantial and that it would not be reasonable to expect that the client would have made a different decision in any relevant respect. The proof of these matters lies with the law practice.
As to the first limb, the steps that were taken were:
(a) providing costs estimates;

(b) providing counsel’s fee disclosures; and

(c) advising that the costs agreement was delayed.

That these constituted reasonable steps was not addressed. The above steps addressed in part the requirements of a costs agreement, and may be seen as steps that partially ensured that the wife was equipped with the information necessary to determine whether she would engage the law practice. However, the information was also significantly deficient for that purpose. It was not shown to encompass advice to the wife as to the manner of charging, or of other matters to be contained in the costs disclosure. The identified steps fell short of what was reasonable.
As to the second limb, a costs disclosure agreement was provided to the wife to rectify the deficit within such a period.
As to the third limb, it may be accepted that the delay beyond “as soon as practicable” was not substantial, leaving the issue as to whether it would not be reasonable to expect that the wife would have taken a different decision. Again, this was a matter not addressed. Perhaps the urgency and intensity of the circumstances may speak to such a result, but they do not automatically do so.
AF Pty Ltd has not established that the Rule 72A exemption applies.
The result then is the application of s 178, resulting in the fee agreement being void, a requirement for assessment, a prohibition on the commencement of proceedings for the recovery of costs prior to assessment, and a potential issue of unsatisfactory professional conduct or professional misconduct.
The consequence is that the charge on the Suburb J property, reliant as it is upon the costs agreement, does not exist.
The issue then as to the litigation being able to proceed for declaratory relief in respect of a lien relies upon the scope of s 178(1)(c), which is discussed in greater detail below in relation to the claims made by ZZ Pty Ltd.

ZZ Pty Ltd’s claim

ZZ Pty Ltd’s claim for relief is set out in an Amended Application in a Proceeding, purportedly filed on 7 March 2025, but not then accepted for filing.
Leave was granted to ZZ Pty Ltd to file the Amended Application in court as identifying the relief sought (Exhibit R14-6). That relief relevantly included declaratory relief as to a lien over the wife’s property, and declaratory relief as to an equitable charge over the Suburb J property.
ZZ Pty Ltd’s primary claims were for summary judgment in its favour in the sum of $466,168.95 and interest, that such be secured by equitable charge upon the Suburb J property, that the Suburb J property be sold and that the claim of ZZ Pty Ltd have priority over all other claims upon that property. ZZ Pty Ltd sought to then be excused from the balance of the proceedings. ZZ Pty Ltd’s claim was not dealt with summarily at the commencement of the hearing.
ZZ Pty Ltd pleads, and the wife concedes, that the wife commenced instructing ZZ Pty Ltd on or around 24 August 2023.
The wife was sent the costs disclosure and agreement on 5 September 2023. She accepts that she entered into the costs agreement. ZZ Pty Ltd asserts that the agreement was dated 12 September 2023 but accepts that the disclosure and agreement were sent again on 14 November 2023, and that a copy signed by the wife was not returned until 10 July 2024.
ZZ Pty Ltd ceased acting for the wife on 17 September 2024.
The wife accepts that a term of the agreement was the granting of a charge over real property owned by the wife, however, the wife denies that ZZ Pty Ltd holds a charge over the Suburb J property.

The wife’s specific complaint in relation to ZZ Pty Ltd

The wife does not pursue a timeliness complaint in respect of being provided with a fee agreement, despite a difference of only a few days as compared with AF Pty Ltd. Rather, she first complains that the costs agreements did not provide estimates as required by the Uniform Law. In particular she asserts that they did not provide an estimate of total legal costs, nor provide a break down as to how it would be calculated in terms of the days or work undertaken in respect of each individual step.
ZZ Pty Ltd asserts that the costs agreement gave a comprehensive estimate of legal costs and provided the relevant notice of dispute mechanisms. ZZ Pty Ltd identifies two estimates given in respect of costs, one being on 5 September 2023, for a limited time period of 30 days at $220,000, and then on 12 September 2023, a more comprehensive estimate for the duration of the proceedings, dependent upon what stage they may resolve, but culminating in an estimate if the matter resolved following a final hearing of $5 million.
Exhibit R14-1 is the costs agreement provided to the wife on 12 September 2023. It sets out the charge rates of the various personnel to be engaged with the case, the manner in which time charging would be conducted, particular specific costs, along with estimates as to the anticipated costs of the proceedings varying in accordance with the stage at which the proceedings might be resolved.
By this ZZ Pty Ltd correctly contends that s 174 of the Uniform Law was complied with and the wife’s first complaint is without merit.
The wife accepts that she was rendered invoices on 25 October 2023 of $43,017.90 and 17 September 2024 of $423,151.05.
The wife did not challenge ZZ Pty Ltd’s contention as to the work undertaken by them.
Accepting that such work was undertaken would lead to prima facie acceptance that, if the costs agreement and the invoices are compliant, the quantum of what is asserted by ZZ Pty Ltd marks out fair and reasonable legal fees owed by the wife.
Within the fee agreement, as ultimately executed by the wife, the wife granted a charge over any real property owned by her to ZZ Pty Ltd and consented to ZZ Pty Ltd lodging a caveat in support of such.
The wife secondly complains that the invoices rendered failed to comply with s 194 of the Uniform Law, by their failure to include on the invoice the avenues open to negotiate or to dispute costs.
ZZ Pty Ltd concedes that the invoices themselves did not contain notices advising the wife of avenues available in the event of a costs dispute.

Discussion ZZ Pty Ltd

Exhibits R14-2 and R14-3 are the invoices rendered by ZZ Pty Ltd to the wife. Neither contain a notification of the wife’s rights as required by s 192 of the Uniform Law. As identified above, s 194 of the Uniform Law places a restriction on proceedings. On its face, s 194 prevents ZZ Pty Ltd from commencing proceedings for the recovery of costs.
ZZ Pty Ltd submits that as a respondent they have not relevantly commenced proceedings. ZZ Pty Ltd reasonably accepted that a cross-claim may be regarded as proceedings, depending upon context. The context here is that ZZ Pty Ltd has initiated an intervention in these proceedings directed to the recovery of legal costs. Despite not being the architects of the original application that was directed to the adjustment of property rights between the parties to the marriage, their joinder and application insofar as it is directed to the recovery of legal costs meets the description at s 194 of commencing proceedings. ZZ Pty Ltd’s application is, subject to other arguments, caught by s 194.
An issue then arises as to what is covered by the term “proceedings to recover legal costs.” There are four elements of the claim made by ZZ Pty Ltd in this litigation. One is as to a liability of the wife to ZZ Pty Ltd in the sum of $466,168.95 plus interest. The second is as to security in relation to the wife’s liability to them (to whatever degree) in the form of a charge in ZZ Pty Ltd’s favour on the Suburb J property. The third is as to the priority to be accorded to that charge, focussing upon competing claims for priority between ZZ Pty Ltd and AF Pty Ltd. The fourth is as to security in relation to the wife’s liability to them (to whatever degree) in the form of a fruits of action lien against proceeds received by the wife in this matter.
The scope of the prohibition depends on the terms of the legislation. Legislative provisions are to be construed in their legislative context and consistently with their legislative purpose. Here the purpose of the provision falls within an integrated scheme for regulating lawyers and costs agreements, ensuring appropriate protections to clients, and rendering the resolution of costs disputes between lawyer and client efficient and fair. Section 194(1) requires strict compliance with the requirement to give particular notice to a client prior to commencing proceedings to recover costs.
It is a restriction that catches ZZ Pty Ltd’s claim for the recovery of $466,168.95 plus interest.
Whether it forms a restriction on proceedings that establish security and/or priority of security is a different question.
The privative nature of the provision points to a construction that resolves ambiguity in favour of access to court.
Although the issue of security is closely related to the recovery of costs, establishing security is distinct from recovery, relating to the capacity to recover rather than the recovery itself.
Establishing the presence of a security does not establish an entitlement in respect of costs. Circumstances where it is necessary to commence proceedings to establish the security even where the liability for costs has not yet been established may be readily envisaged, this case forming an example of such, seen not only in the application by ZZ Pty Ltd, but also in the application by AF Pty Ltd.
Section 194 should not be construed to restrict ZZ Pty Ltd’s (or AF Pty Ltd’s) application insofar as it relates to security. It, if applicable to these proceedings, does prevent the application insofar as it relates to the recovery of $466,168.95 plus interest.
The next issue pursued by ZZ Pty Ltd is as to whether the NSW state enactment embodied in s 194 of the Uniform Law has application in these federal proceedings. If ZZ Pty Ltd is correct in its contention that it does not, there is no bar to ZZ Pty Ltd’s claim not only as to security, but also as to the debt itself.
That is an issue governed by s 79(1) of the Judiciary Act 1903 (Cth) (‘the Judiciary Act’) which is in the following terms:
The laws of each State or Territory, including the laws relating to procedure, evidence, and the competency of witnesses, shall, except as otherwise provided by the Constitution or the laws of the Commonwealth, be binding on all Courts exercising federal jurisdiction in that State or Territory in all cases to which they are applicable.
In Rizeq v Western Australia [2017] HCA 23; (2017) 262 CLR 1 (“Rizeq”) the plurality described the interaction of State law and the exercise by a court of federal jurisdiction, and the work of s 79 of the Judiciary Act in the following compact manner (at [103]):
Within the limits of State legislative capacity, State laws apply in federal jurisdiction as valid State laws unless and to the extent that they are rendered invalid by reason of inconsistency with Commonwealth laws. What State laws relevantly cannot do within the limits of State legislative capacity is govern the exercise by a court of federal jurisdiction. A State law can determine neither the powers that a court has in the exercise of federal jurisdiction nor how or in what circumstances those powers are to be exercised. A State law cannot in that sense “bind” a court in the exercise of federal jurisdiction, and that is the sense in which that word is used in s 79 of the Judiciary Act. The operation of s 79 is limited to making the text of the State laws of that nature apply as Commonwealth law to bind a court in the exercise of federal jurisdiction.
In the determination of this matter, as well as applying the Family Law Act, the court applies State laws that “form part of the single composite body of federal and non-federal law that is applicable”[21] in order to resolve the rights and liabilities of the parties.
Insofar as the State law is directed to the rights and liabilities of the parties, for example, in respect of their rights and liabilities conferred by the Uniform Law, the State law has direct operation.
To the extent that the Uniform Law, as State law, purports to determine how a court will exercise federal jurisdiction, including in relation to the matters governed by the Uniform Law, it cannot directly do so. That work is done by s 79 of the Judiciary Act. At [87] the plurality in Rizeq described:
“Characteristically an exercise of jurisdiction is attended by an exercise of power”. By making State laws that are “binding” on courts also binding on courts exercising federal jurisdiction, s 79 of the Judiciary Act takes the text of State laws conferring or governing powers that State courts have when exercising State jurisdiction and applies that text as Commonwealth law to confer or govern powers that State courts and federal courts have when exercising federal jurisdiction.

(Footnote omitted)
The effect then, is that s 194 of the Uniform Law only applies because it is adopted by s 79 of the Judiciary Act as a federal law governing the manner of exercise of jurisdiction by this court, subject to either the Constitution or laws of the Commonwealth providing otherwise. This conclusion forecloses a further issue of inconsistency raised by ZZ Pty Ltd in the event that s 194 applies of its own force. It does not.
The remaining issue raised by ZZ Pty Ltd is the contention that s 79 of the Family Law Act provides otherwise so as to result in s 79 of the Judiciary Act not picking up s 194 of the Uniform Law.
This argument relates to the character of the s 79 Family Law Act proceedings. ZZ Pty Ltd asserts that s 79 of the Family Law Act provides a “comprehensive regime for the determination of matrimonial disputes.”
The argument is predicated on the proposition that the effect of the application of s 194, even if it does not exclude the determination of the security issue, is to “bifurcate the complete determination of the matrimonial cause”.[22] ZZ Pty Ltd submits that s 79 of the Family Law Act is intended to “completely resolve these sorts of disputes and because of that it’s a comprehensive regime”.[23] ZZ Pty Ltd draws further upon s 81 of the Family Law Act, on the basis that s 81 requires the court to make all such orders as it can in order to completely resolve the dispute. ZZ Pty Ltd points to the unattractive spectre of the quantum of costs issues having to be dealt with in proceedings in another court, and that failure to resolve the extent of the debts associated with the charges upon the Suburb J property would fail to resolve the s 79 Family Law Act dispute.
As described by the High Court in Stanford, s 79 of the Family Law Act requires this court to determine the legal and equitable interests held by the parties to the marriage as a first step in determining what if any adjustment of those interests is just and equitable.
However, neither ss 79 nor 81 of the Family Law Act are directed to the determination of the rights of third parties. Section 81 is directed to finally determining the financial relationships between the parties to the marriage. Section 79 is directed to the property of the parties to the marriage (or to the property of a party that has vested in a trustee in bankruptcy, or in limited circumstances to the child of a marriage). Third party interests are determined either as between the parties to the marriage, or between the parties to the marriage and the third party, as an incident of such.
ZZ Pty Ltd was joined to the proceedings, on ZZ Pty Ltd’s application, on the basis that their claim against the wife formed part of a single justiciable controversy with the s 79 Family Law Act proceedings of the parties to the marriage, entwined as it was with the determination of their property interests.
It is true that the issue pursued by ZZ Pty Ltd is connected to the s 79 Family Law Act dispute. The manner in which the parties’ property is subject, by means of security, to the rights of other parties, forms part of the first step set out in Stanford.
However, while identification of the limitations of the parties’ property rights and associated loans forms a part of that first step, it is an aspect of the proceedings that frequently does not directly involve the third parties whose securities are at issue.
