This can be seen in the ordinary course of property proceedings where evidence of debts is adduced without those debts being pursued within the proceedings by a third party. Typical examples of such include evidence of mortgages and associated loans secured against a family home. Typically the determination as between the parties of their property rights does not involve the intervention of the bank in determining those rights as between the parties to the marriage. Similarly, other unsecured debts may also be the subject of evidence adduced in proceedings without the intervention of the creditor.
While s 79 proceedings may carry the prospect of trampling upon the rights of a creditor in the process of adjusting property interests between the parties to the marriage, the resolution of a dispute between the parties to the marriage in relation to a particular debt does not, unless the creditor is a party to the proceedings, or accorded the opportunity to be a party to the proceedings, resolve the rights of the creditor. Typically those rights are determined only as between the parties to the marriage on the evidence presented at trial.
Fulfilling the requirement of s 79 as described in Stanford does not necessitate that the third party be able to enforce their right in the proceedings in this court. An incapacity on the part of ZZ Pty Ltd to pursue their claims does not defeat the operation of s 79.
To the extent that ZZ Pty Ltd contends that it must be able to prosecute its debt claim for the court to resolve the s 79 issue, this is not so.
The Commonwealth law does not otherwise provide in a manner that displaces the picking up of the provision in the Uniform Law that restrains ZZ Pty Ltd from commencing enforcement proceedings for its fees.
ZZ Pty Ltd is correct to observe that practical difficulty accompanies such an outcome. Not only is there the spectre of further proceedings in another jurisdiction, but the assessment of what is just and equitable between the parties is hampered where there is a lack of clarity as to the quantum of the indebtedness of the wife.
In any event, even if there were not such a restriction, despite the evidence adduced in the proceedings, the level of indebtedness to ZZ Pty Ltd is unable to be determined. Given the wife’s concessions as to the completion of work by ZZ Pty Ltd, there is an associated debt. However, the reasonableness of the fees were challenged by the wife, and the underlying evidence did not give clarity as to whether the fees charged were in accordance with the fee agreement, depriving ZZ Pty Ltd’s assertion of quantum of the benefit accorded by s 172(4) of the Uniform Law that the fees charged are prima facie fair and reasonable.
While it may be the case that such is established in other proceedings, it is not established here, resulting in an inability to determine the wife’s indebtedness. In the context of the terms of s 79 in relation to this hearing, such does not preclude the court from resolving the s 79 dispute between the husband and the wife.
Perhaps in this case the inability to determine the quantum of the wife’s debt is of little consequence, relating as it does to the wife’s own costs, for which she generally would have responsibility, subject to a successful costs application on her part.
What is however established is that ZZ Pty Ltd holds a charge on the Suburb J property, and also, subject to there being fruits of the action, a fruits of action lien in respect of proceeds from these proceedings.
A further issue arose of priority, ZZ Pty Ltd asserting priority over the claim by AF Pty Ltd, and over the claim by Ms OO.
The first contention by ZZ Pty Ltd is that the charge granted by the fee agreement has priority over any fruits of action lien accrued by Ms OO, as the lien only comes into effect after the charge, crystallising on the judgment being entered. This proposition may be accepted.
ZZ Pty Ltd further contends that the charge created by the fee agreement entered into with the wife has priority over the corresponding charge purportedly granted by the wife to AF Pty Ltd in that earlier fee agreement with AF Pty Ltd. Given the fee agreement between the wife and AF Pty Ltd is void, no charge is created for AF Pty Ltd.

Ms OO’s claim

As identified above, the claim in respect of Ms OO’s purported representation of the wife is mounted by Ms OO (the seventh respondent) who works for AO Lawyers and AN Pty Ltd (the fifteenth respondent), being the entity through which Ms OO operates AO Lawyers.
The relief sought by Ms OO was directed to a declaration as to a lien over the jewellery, restraints on the handling of the jewellery, that the proceeds from the sale of the jewellery be held on trust pending further order, and costs on an indemnity basis.[24]
There was significant controversy in relation to Ms OO’s claims, including as to whether Ms OO was retained by the wife at all.
Ms OO relied upon a purported fee agreement dated 20 November 2016 (Exhibit R7-1). That fee agreement was not signed by Ms OO.
In her affidavit filed 13 March 2025, Ms OO described that she mailed the agreement to the B Pty Ltd office in Suburb PP. Ms OO then became aware that the wife had left for Country P. She did not check with the wife whether she received it.[25]
It was uncontroversial that the wife has not executed the purported agreement. The wife denies having received the document.
Ms OO was challenged as to whether the document had in fact been sent, responding “I sent it, I sent it, 100 percent, but your client doesn’t read. That’s her usual story...she doesn’t sign documents”.[26] This consciousness on the part of Ms OO sits poorly with the obligation cast upon her by the Uniform Law to ensure that a client understands the basis for charging.
Ms OO described that, in the various work undertaken by her for the wife, incorporating immigration work (apparently related to either the husband’s business or the parties’ charity) the wife had never signed a fee agreement.[27]
No indication was given by Ms OO that there had been any contact between herself and the wife identifying that the wife had actually received the document or understood its content.
Ms OO however asserts that the wife is bound by the terms of the costs agreement because the wife continued to provide instruction following its receipt, with such mode of acceptance set out at section M of the agreement in the following terms:[28]
M. ACCEPTANCE OF OFFER

You may accept the Costs Disclosure and Costs Agreement by:

M1 Signing and returning this document to us or:

M2 Continuing to instruct us. Upon acceptance you agree to pay for our services on these terms
This effect relies upon two underlying propositions. The first is that it was received by the wife. The second is that the costs agreement is applicable.
As to the first, Ms OO has not demonstrated that the wife relevantly received the document. The document, at best, was sent to the husband’s business at about the time the wife travelled overseas. Even if it was accepted that the document was sent as described, there can be no satisfaction that the wife received it or was aware of its content.
It may also be observed that the disclosure obligations at s 174(3) of the Uniform Law require the following:
Client’s consent and understanding If a disclosure is made under subsection (1), the law practice must take all reasonable steps to satisfy itself that the client has understood and given consent to the proposed course of action for the conduct of the matter and the proposed costs.
No reasonable steps were identified as having been taken.
Secondly, even if the agreement had been entered into by the wife, it would be void.
The scope of work to be covered by the purported agreement is unclear, indicating work potentially to be undertaken in the Federal Magistrates Court and Family Court (if necessary) but also in Country P in relation to the husband’s divorce there.
Importantly, the wife identifies that the purported agreement does not comply with the requirements of the Uniform Law and asserts that it is rendered void by that non-compliance. In particular, the asserted costs agreement contained no estimate as required by s 174 of the Uniform Law.
Ms OO asserts that the wife instructed her to perform work in accordance with the costs agreement in Australia and Country P.
Exhibit R7-2 purports to be a covering letter addressed to the wife at UU Street, Suburb PP relating to the fee agreement, also dated 20 November 2016, identifying a retainer, for the wife’s “Family Matter” yet also for the husband’s divorce from his first wife in Country P, and the registration of the husband’s and wife’s marriage in Country P, along with the registration of the parties’ children in Country P.
However, absent an estimate, the costs agreement is void pursuant to s 178(1) of the Unform Law and restrains the legal practice, as described earlier, from commencing or maintaining proceedings for the recovery of costs until assessment has taken place.
No contention was raised that the voiding effect of the failure to estimate costs is displaced by operation of Rule 72A of the LPUGR. None could reasonably have been raised.
As discussed in relation to ZZ Pty Ltd, the relief sought here, insofar as it is limited to the identification of a lien, does not fall foul of the restriction in s 178 of the Uniform Law, restricted as it is to the identification of security rather than the recovery of costs.

Was Ms OO the wife’s lawyer?

The first hurdle to be crossed by Ms OO is as to whether she was the wife’s lawyer, this matter, somewhat unusually, being contentious.
While the wife appeared to accept that she had received some advice from Ms OO, and that she had received support and assistance from Ms OO, she denied that Ms OO did so as her lawyer. Instead, she asserted that Ms OO acted in her capacity as a friend to help the wife and husband “sort out marital issues” by acting “as a mediator or intermediary”.[29]
Perhaps adding some credence to the wife’s claim is that Ms OO accepted that she rendered no invoice to the wife prior to the one currently relied upon by her, despite, on her account, having purportedly acted for the wife for a period of November 2016 to April 2023.
While it might be thought that a contest as to whether Ms OO was the wife’s lawyer would be readily answerable by Ms OO, Ms OO explained that she was limited in the material that she could provide by reason of her laptop having crashed, resulting in the loss of all emails, but the retention of other files.
Further, Ms OO’s attempts to tender documents that, on her account, must be protected by privilege, were refused.
In that context there was limited evidence produced by Ms OO.
Adding further complexity to the issue, the wife contends that if the court finds that there was a solicitor-client relationship, it was one that was conflicted. The wife asserts this conflict arises in circumstances where Ms OO had conducted work for the husband and for B Pty Ltd, Ms OO had undertaken work in respect of the registration of the husband’s divorce from his previous wife in Country P, and further that a solicitor-client relationship was inconsistent with particular conduct by Ms OO, as set out below.
The nature of Ms OO’s dealings with the wife were clouded by the nature of her relationships with both parties to the marriage, and by her conduct. The wife described that Ms OO would say to her “I was the ambassador of peace I am helping a friend”.[30] The wife says that she thought that Ms OO was acting as an intermediary as a family friend. It may be noted that whatever Ms OO did as a lawyer for the wife, it did not involve commencing proceedings, which may have lent more clarity to the nature of their relationship.
Prior to the end of the relationship Ms OO socialised with the wife and husband, having, for example, invited them both to a dinner party. The wife described Ms OO’s regular attendance at the family home for dinner and sheesha. While socialising between a lawyer and client does not itself negative a professional relationship, the social nature of the relationship explains the wife’s claims that Ms OO was helping her as a friend. Again, friendship with a lawyer does not of itself negate a professional relationship, but it is a matter apt to cloud the basis of interaction, particularly where the assistance (or at least parts of the assistance) appears non-legal in nature. For example, Ms OO concedes that she liaised with clergy to assist in the resolution of the dispute, further asserting that this was not incorporated in the invoice she ultimately rendered. Ms OO, however, denies that her involvement with the wife was in the realm of mediation.
One issue that arose was her collaboration with the husband.
Ms OO had previously undertaken work in relation to visas and had been paid for such work by the corporate entities controlled by the husband. Ms OO described that she has acted on instructions from the wife “in numerous Immigration Matters relating to [BK Foundation] of which she was the Director and CEO”.[31] Although Ms OO styled these as on the instructions of the wife, it cannot be concluded that Ms OO’s retainer was with the wife, rather than with entities controlled by the husband.
Ms OO was also heavily involved in legal matters in Country P concerning the husband’s earlier marriage and wife and then registration of the husband’s and wife’s marriage. Although Ms OO styled these proceedings as for the wife, I do not accept such a characterisation. Accepting that the wife may have been interested in the pursuit and outcome of such proceedings, and directly involved in the registration of her own marriage and children, such does not equate to the wife being in a solicitor client relationship with Ms OO insofar as it related to the husband’s previous marriage. Rather it is suggestive that Ms OO was engaged in the pursuit of the husband’s interests and claims, the pursuit of which were terminated by the husband.
Ms OO tendered two documents regarding the progression of the Country P work. Evidently work was undertaken in relation to the Country P matters.
Further issues as to collaboration with the husband arose as, during a period in which Ms OO purported to act for the wife, she was involved, in the wife’s absence, in a video communication between the husband and one of the parties’ children, Y. Ms OO attended the wife’s home in her absence, asserting that the mother had consented to her doing so in order to pray with a priest with the children. Whilst there, Ms OO called the husband. She then described Y taking the phone into another part of the house.
Part way through the call Ms OO can be seen in the presence of Y. The husband instructed Ms OO to listen to Y “what mummy told him to lie.” Y then said that when he attended a therapist that mum made him to say that dad hit her while she was in bed with Y. When the father then asked Y what the truth was and whether he had ever hit the mother, Y replied “I don’t know, maybe”. The father repeated “do I ever hit your mum” to which Y replied “no” and the father then demanded to know why Y lied and why the mother made her lie and that Y had told him upstairs that the mother was blackmailing him. The father then said, “so you know that mum blackmailing me” to which Y responded “she is.” Ms OO then spoke in another language. She said this was in Arabic and was telling the husband to stop. Further comments were then made by the father in another language. The end of the conversation was not produced. Ms OO appeared to be holding the phone that Y was using for at least part of the conversation (Exhibit A3).
It was put to Ms OO that this interaction was an attempt by her to obtain evidence to benefit the husband. Ms OO denied this, asserting that she told the father to stop in the Arabic language. No explanation was given as to why Ms OO had called the husband, or given the phone to the child, or participated by her attendance in the egregious cross examination of the child by the father.
Ms OO further provided pictures of the wife to the husband. The husband denied that he had sought that Ms OO provide him with photos of the wife. He says that Ms OO provided him with photos to convince him that the wife was at home. Ms OO asserted to the wife that she asked the nanny to tell the wife that the husband was angry with her, accusing the wife of infidelity.
Exhibit R7-19 is a file note of 13 May 2023 where Ms OO explains why she sent a picture of the wife to the husband. This appeared to follow discussion between Ms OO and the husband, the husband wanting to know the whereabouts of the wife, and wanting to speak to the wife. The wife had responded that she was having a massage, and Ms OO requested a photo of the person doing the massage. A photo was taken by the maid which Ms OO forwarded to the husband, on the assumption that the wife intended this to occur.
More consistent with the work of a lawyer, Ms OO claims to have acted for the wife in “family law matters and negotiations including court proceedings in [Country P]...general advice about her family law matter, custody of her three children, her rights in [Country P], and advice on Binding Financial Agreement(s) and instructing counsel ([Mr AZ]) to provide advice on family law property matters, Caveat, and Binding Financial Agreement, and advice on her Criminal Matter”.[32]
Ms OO produced material suggestive that she engaged a barrister on behalf of the wife. An affidavit of Mr AZ, barrister, confirmed that, at some unidentified stage, Ms OO approached him for advice regarding the marriage breakdown between the wife and husband. He says that he was introduced to the wife by Ms OO on 7 February 2023, and described to the wife being able to provide advice to her to enable financial support whilst the parties were still on reasonable terms. He describes a single conference with the wife of 4 March 2023.
Mr AZ described rendering a number of invoices through April to June 2023, describing none to have been paid by the wife, but that Ms OO made part payment in any event. Mr AZ then offered gratuitous negative opinion as to the wife’s character.
A further contentious involvement of Ms OO was in relation to the removal of the wife’s jewellery from her home into the custody of Ms OO. Ms OO produced file notes from 17 March 2023 of interactions with the wife regarding the husband’s causing of the jewellery to be removed from the home, including observing the return of the jewellery, the wife’s comments that Ms OO needed to be paid for her work as her lawyer, and Ms OO being given permission by the husband’s lawyer to negotiate with him directly. She also described her car being parked in a manner that happened to block the wife from driving to the police station to report the jewellery missing.
The husband appeared to concede that he had arranged for the wife’s jewellery to be removed from the house, justified by the wife giving her jewellery to her boyfriend. The jewellery was shortly thereafter returned to the wife.
Ms OO described that the wife deposited the jewellery with Ms OO saying that she did so to secure Ms OO’s costs on 21 April 2023. Ms OO says that the wife was given the only keys to the lockbox, but later Ms OO caused the lockbox to be opened by a locksmith and transferred the jewellery to AB Pty Ltd. Ms OO claims a lien upon the jewellery.
The wife says that Ms OO told her that the husband had contacted her to say that he had taken the wife’s jewellery and was going to give it to drug dealers. The wife says that she discovered the jewellery gone and threatened that she would call the police. The jewellery was then returned to the wife in the presence of Ms OO and the husband’s then lawyer.
The wife says that on 21 April 2023 Ms OO recommended that the jewellery be placed into a safe deposit box to prevent the husband from taking it again. Ms OO denied that this incident was an artifice created by her to enable her to possess the jewellery.
The wife says that when she asked for the jewellery to be returned on 30 April 2023 Ms OO refused.
At Exhibit R7-10 Ms OO produced a file note dated 30 April 2023 of a conversation with the wife where the wife accepted that Ms OO needed to be paid, that the husband should pay those fees, and expressing that the jewellery had been hidden at Ms OO’s home to keep it safe, disputing that the jewellery should be held as a ransom for legal fees.
Exhibit R7-11 contains communication from the wife to Ms OO of 30 April 2023, where the wife reiterates that the jewellery was retained for safekeeping by Ms OO, that she had learnt from “[Mr BA]” (AF Pty Ltd) of the lien and the intent of Ms OO to use the jewellery as collateral for legal fees. The wife asked Ms OO not to hold the jewellery in such a manner. The wife further expressed her gratitude for Ms OO’s “unwavering support” and acknowledged her efforts in working “collaboratively”. The wife observed that the “pre-settlement agreement” with the husband was for him to meet Ms OO’s fees.
Ms OO relied upon particular material to support her status as the wife’s lawyer. Exhibit R7-13 contains correspondence form the wife to the husband of 11 April 2023 where the wife asserts that Ms OO will soon send a pre settlement agreement (whatever that might be) to the husband.
Exhibit R7-17 is an invoice prepared by Ms OO of 26 April 2023. Although it identifies categories of work purportedly undertaken it ultimately identifies the fee in a merely conclusory manner, in the sum of $495,000 (GST incl). It contains entries such as “any other work as the case may require”, “obtaining your future instructions”, “obtaining other work as the case may require” which, on their face, appear to relate to work not yet undertaken. It also referred to having attended with the wife on numerous occasions on various legal practitioners, including counsel appearing at the trial for Ms OO, a Mr Foster. It appeared uncontentious that this was false, and that the wife never attended upon that counsel.
Exhibit R7-18 is a file note by Ms OO of 5 May 2023 referring to a message sent by the wife saying “as you said : you are my lawyer”.
Exhibit R7-25 is a file note of 11 May 2023 giving an account of Ms OO’s attendance with the priest at the wife’s home in her absence. Ms OO described the intervention of the priest advising the wife to resolve the matter on a religious basis rather than through a court.
Exhibit R7-24 comprised 47 pages of partial interactions between the wife, the husband and Ms OO spanning March 2020 through to May 2023. Some of the interactions are suggestive of Ms OO functioning as the wife’s lawyer rather than as her friend and confidante.
It may be accepted that there was a confused relationship between Ms OO and the wife, but a relationship in which Ms OO provided some legal services to the wife, potentially to the husband regarding his divorce in Country P, along with migration work associated with the parties and their corporate entities.
Appropriately both parties recognised that solicitors have a fiduciary duty to their clients. That is indeed the case. A fundamental requirement of that duty is that solicitors must avoid being in a position of actual or potential conflict of interest with those of their clients.
The key issue raised by the wife is whether Ms OO’s conduct with respect to the husband, and in the context of her prior work for B Pty Ltd, was inconsistent with the duty of loyalty that was owed to the wife if in fact Ms OO was her lawyer. That is also an issue that arises in the circumstances of Ms OO’s retention of the wife’s jewellery.
One aspect of that duty of loyalty was described by McClelland J in Badawi & Badawi [2016] FamCA 804 (from [92]):
Appropriately both parties recognised that solicitors have a fiduciary duty to their clients. That is indeed the case. A fundamental requirement of that duty is that solicitors must avoid being in a position of actual or potential conflict of interest with those of their clients.
Once Ms O accepted instructions from the husband, she was in a fiduciary relationship with him.
In Law Society of New South Wales v Harvey [1976] 2 NSWLR 154, the NSW Court of Appeal said at 170-171:
Where there is any conflict between the interest of the client and that of the solicitor, the duty of the solicitor is to act in perfect good faith and to make full disclosure of his interest. It must be a conscientious disclosure of all material circumstances, and everything known to him relating to the proposed transaction which might influence the conduct of the client or anybody from whom he might seek advice. To disclose less than all that is material may positively mislead. Thus for a solicitor merely to disclose that he has an interest, without identifying the interest, may serve only to mislead the client into an enhanced confidence that the solicitor will be in a position better to protect the client’s interest. The conflict of interest may, and usually will, be such that it is not proper, or even possible, for the solicitor to continue to act for and advise his client. A solicitor, who deals with his client while remaining his solicitor, undertakes a heavy burden. Where a solicitor discovers that continuing to act for his client will, or may, bring the interests of his client and his own interests into conflict, it will be a rare case where he should not, at least, advise his client to take independent legal advice. It may well happen that the conflict arises fortuitously, and has not been anticipated when the solicitor undertook to act for the client. This circumstance does not alter the duty of the solicitor already referred to.
In Howard v Commissioner of Taxation [2014] HCA 21 at [59], Hayne and Crennan JJ summarised a fiduciary obligation to avoid a conflict of interest in the following terms:

It is well established that “[i]t is an inflexible rule of a Court of Equity that a person in a fiduciary position ... is not, unless otherwise expressly provided, entitled to make a profit; he is not allowed to put himself in a position where his interest and duty conflict”. The majority in Pilmar v Duke Group Ltd (in liq) said of this obligation that “the fiduciary is under an obligation, without informed consent, not to promote the personal interests of the fiduciary by making or pursuing a gain in circumstances in which there is ‘a conflict or a real or substantial possibility of a conflict’ between personal interests of the fiduciary and those to whom the duty of owed”.
(References omitted)
Similarly, in Bristol and West Building Society v Mothew [1998] Ch 1 Millett LJ held that:
The distinguishing obligation of a fiduciary is the obligation of loyalty. The principal is entitled to the single-minded loyalty of his fiduciary. This core liability has several facets. A fiduciary must act in good faith; he must not make a profit out of his trust; he must not place himself in a position where his duty and his interest may conflict; he may not act for his own benefit or for the benefit of a third person without the informed consent of his principal. This is not intended to be an exhaustive list, but it is sufficient to indicate the nature of the fiduciary obligation. They are the defining characteristics of the fiduciary.
(Emphasis added)
(Footnote omitted)
The instances illustrating the asserted breach of the duty of loyalty firstly were directed at the previous representation in relation to B Pty Ltd and the associated charity, Ms OO’s fees having been met, effectively, by the husband. The representation appears to have related to migration issues. In the absence of something more particular, this prior circumstance does not demonstrate a breach of loyalty inconsistent with Ms OO being the wife’s lawyer.
The second relates to Ms OO’s dealings with the husband, in particular her forwarding of images of the wife to the husband, and her facilitation of and participation in the video call with Y. Whatever criticism may be quite legitimately levelled at Ms OO in respect of these matters, it should not be concluded that they were grounded in Ms OO acting for the benefit of the husband. However ill-advised such conduct might be, it does not go so far as to demonstrate inconsistency with a solicitor-client relationship. Lastly in relation to the husband, a question arises as to the significance of the Country P proceedings. However, without a further exploration of those issues it should not be concluded that Ms OO then acting for the wife against the husband was inconsistent with the pursuit of the husband’s divorce in Country P.
The third aspect related to Ms OO’s retention of the wife’s jewellery. As noted above the wife’s contention was that Ms OO had manufactured or preyed upon the circumstances in order to retain her jewellery, matters denied by Ms OO. The purported fee agreement of 20 November 2016 is relied upon by Ms OO (Exhibit R7-1). It contained the following provisions to provide security for legal fees from the wife:
W LIEN

Without affecting any lien to which this firm otherwise entitled at law over funds, papers and other property pertaining to you in this firm’s possession:
(a) This firm shall be entitled to retain by way of lien any funds, property or papers of yours, which are from time to time in our possession or control, until all costs, disbursements, interest and other moneys due to the firm have been paid; and

(b) This firm shall irrevocably charge all estates or interests in real estate which you, or any company in relation to which you have authority to exercise the power to charge real estate, now have or may hereafter acquire with payment of all moneys that may be or become due to this firm under this costs agreement” (THE “CHARGING CLAUSE”).

(c) This firm’s lien will continue notwithstanding that we cease to act for you.
Given that this purported fee agreement is void, the lien clause will have no effect.
However, Ms OO purports to exercise a lien in respect of the jewellery on the basis that it came into her possession. The wife complains that Ms OO took the jewellery into her possession for safekeeping, and now seeks to retain it for another purpose, being security for legal fees.
The wife and Ms OO are at odds over whether the wife was on notice of Ms OO’s intent to hold the jewellery as security for her fees. Ms OO asserted that the wife said to her “I will deposit this jewellery to secure your costs. I am scared that my husband may steal it as he did before”.[33] She describes that the wife also said “He will take the Jewellery and he means it. What about if he does not pay you? I have to secure your costs and my jewellery is the only security for your costs. I will sell the Jewellery to pay your Costs. Please help me keep it in a safe place”.[34] Further she says that the wife said “I will ask my husband to pay your costs and if he refuses, I shall sell some jewellery and pay you costs”.[35]
The wife described that on 21 April 2023, Ms OO asked to meet at BL Bank to place the Jewellery in a safe box at the bank, saying that "[Mr Pirani] will send people to your house to take your belonging. Let's put them in a safe. I will come with you".[36]
The wife said that the bank sought a deposit of $30,000.00 to store the jewellery, which the wife could not meet. She described that Ms OO said ''I will take the jewellery and the kids’ passports and keep them in the safe box at my house".[37]
Importantly, Ms OO also records 2 messages sent by the wife proximate to the removal of the jewellery that sit well with the wife’s claim that she was not put on notice by Ms OO that the jewellery was to be held as security, or as the wife describes it “ransom” for the legal fees.
Exhibit R7-11, being a transcript of a WhatsApp chat sent from the wife on 30 April 2023, included: ‘[Mr BA] [the wife’s lawyer from AF Pty Ltd] just explain to me that the Lien trust is for you to hold my jewelry [sic] as a ransom for your legal fees’.
Exhibit R7-10, being file notes written by Ms OO, again on 30 April 2023, describe a recorded message sent by the wife: “I do not wanna feel that the agreement between me and you that I give you the jewellery to hold them for ransom for my legal fees because that is not fair and that is not what happened. What happened is I was really worried about my jewellery been stolen again, from me so we went to your house and we’ve hidden it at your house for safety reasons not more than that” [sic].
On Ms OO’s description, although the jewellery was retained on her premises, she, initially, did not exercise control over such, all keys being given to the wife. That lack of control appears consistent with the jewellery being retained for the purpose of its safekeeping. It was converted into actual control when Ms OO used a locksmith to enable her to take that jewellery into her actual possession.
I do not accept that the wife was told that the jewellery was to be held as security for Ms OO’s fees. The wife’s surprise at such an outcome, discovered from speaking with her subsequent solicitor, is evident. The retention of the jewellery was very much in Ms OO’s interest if a possessory lien was to be claimed over it, with such interest conflicting with the interests of the wife.
However, even accepting that such conduct is inconsistent with the heavy obligations placed upon a fiduciary does not mean that the conduct negates the relationship of solicitor-client.
Overall it may be concluded that a portion of the interactions with, and interventions by Ms OO were not in the nature of work as the wife’s lawyer. A number were non-legal in nature. A number in relation to the Country P proceedings were apparently for the benefit of the husband. A number were ill advised, for example her participation in the call with Y, and the interactions regarding the jewellery are not consistent with her obligations to the wife. However, the wife at times made representations that Ms OO was her lawyer and apparently relied upon her as such. At times Ms OO functioned as the wife’s lawyer, for example in the retention of counsel. While the solicitor-client aspect of the relationship does not explain the whole of their interactions, it represents a part of their interactions. That is, there was a solicitor-client relationship, although the extent is far from clear. The extent is not illuminated by the rendering of the invoice by Ms OO.

The effect of the purported charges

Given the void nature of the AF Pty Ltd fee agreement, and the Ms OO fee agreement, the only remaining contractual charge relates to the costs agreement with ZZ Pty Ltd.
That fee agreement purports to create a charge in favour of the legal practice, being a charge upon the property of the wife. As identified in respect of building contracts by Mossop M in Adrija Pty Ltd v Mohamed & Ors [2013] ACTSC 120 at [22], “there is no doubt that charging clauses in building contracts can give rise to interests which justify the maintenance of a caveat.” Further, the effect of the clause is to be determined by consideration “of the terms of the clause as a whole”.[38]
In Danthanarayana v GR8 Constructions Pty Ltd [2012] FCA 231, in the context of a building contract that granted a charge upon the land the subject of the work, Foster J adopted the following at [45]:
At p 196, the authors of Sykes and Walker, The Law of Securities, said:
The only actual requirements of the equitable charge seem to be, first, intention; secondly, if over land, the presence of writing; thirdly, the existence of definite ascertainable property, even though future, over which it is contemplated that the charge shall exist; and lastly, in a few exceptional cases the presence of consideration; consideration would not save a purely oral agreement.
Here, the contract evidences intention to create a charge, expressed wholly in writing.
As to the effect of an equitable charge created by contract, Foster J observed such is created when the property is either expressly or constructively made liable to the discharge of the debt. The right then conferred is to a right of realisation by judicial process, being by the appointment of a receiver or an order for the sale of the property.
Accordingly, in that limited sense, ZZ Pty Ltd’s rights attach to the property of the wife.

The effect of the liens

The further claims are in relation to liens.
In Re Wedgwood: Ex parte Bank of New Zealand [1993] FCA 368; (1993) 116 ALR 153 at [16] Cooper J identified two forms of lien available to a solicitor:
There are two circumstances in which the question of a solicitor’s lien may arise...The first is the right of a solicitor to retain property already in his possession until the solicitor is paid the professional costs due to the solicitor in his or her professional capacity...This “retaining” lien extends to money held in the solicitor’s bank account on behalf of the client...The second right is one to ask the court to direct that personal property recovered by the solicitor’s exertions stand as security for payment of the solicitor’s costs
Justice Cooper observed that the retaining lien gives the solicitor the right to retain possession of an item, a lien or right that is discharged by payment.
Ms OO claims, first, a possessory lien in relation to the jewellery taken into her possession ostensibly for their safe keeping from the husband.
The correspondence between the wife and Ms OO reveals that shortly after Ms OO retained the jewellery it became clear to the wife that it was being held as ransom for Ms OO’s payment. When Ms OO took the jewellery, she did not identify this matter to the wife, the wife discovering such complication after the fact from AF Pty Ltd.[39] That is, Ms OO did not advise the wife of Ms OO’s interest in holding the jewellery. What was represented to the wife as protective of the jewellery had the effect of giving Ms OO the jewellery as security.
I do not accept that a possessory lien arises in either of those circumstances. If Ms OO’s description is correct, she was not granted possession of the items whilst the wife retained all keys. To take possession, without consent or right does not result in a lien.
Even if that is wrong, the possessory lien arises from the solicitor client relationship. As identified above that is a fiduciary relationship that requires a solicitor not to act in conflict of interest. Even if Ms OO’s denials that her retention of the jewellery to protect it was an artifice are accepted, the conflict remains. I do not accept that a possessory lien arises in those circumstances.
The second form of lien, sometimes known as a fruits of action lien, was described by Campbell J in Firth v Centrelink [2002] NSWSC 564; (2002) 55 NSWLR 451 (“Firth”) , adopting as a description of the right what was said by Jordan CJ in Ex Parte Patience; Makinson v The Minister [1940] NSWStRp 11; (1940) 40 SR (NSW) 96, as being an equitable right for a lawyer to be paid from monies to be paid as a result of a judgment in favour of the lawyer’s client, where the lawyer acted for the client in those legal proceedings. Justice Campbell identified a number of features of such liens, salient of which are as follows (at [35]):
(a) The right exists over money recovered through obtaining judgment in litigation or by settlement of litigation;

(b) The right exists over money in the solicitor’s possession, money in court, or money owed to the client but not paid into court;

(c) The solicitor need not still be acting for the client;

(d) “For the right to arise it must be shown that there is a sufficient causal link between solicitor’s exertions and the recovery of the fund of money”;

(e) The quantum to which the right attaches is the amount properly owing and, where taxation is required, the right exists prior to the taxation taking place; and

(f) The right exists prior to the court being asked to intervene to protect it, arising immediately on the recovery of monies, or at the time of the making of an order for costs.

However, Campbell J observed that his description of liens did not consider “the extent to which such a lien might exist over property other than money, or the extent to which a lien might attach to property which was preserved (rather than recovered) through the efforts of the solicitor”.[40]
As to this last point, in Carew Counsel Pty Ltd v French [2002] VSCA 1 Winneke P observed at [33] that the “ essence of the solicitor’s ‘particular’ or ‘non-possessory’ costs lien is that the solicitor has been instrumental in recovering the judgment sum (whether by compromise or otherwise) or, put another way, that the monies have been recovered as a result of (the solicitor’s) exertions...for the right to arise it must be shown that there is a sufficient causal link between the solicitor’s exertions and the recovery of the fund.”
As to causal link, in Color Point Pty Ltd v Markby’s Communication Group Pty Ltd and Ors [1998] FCA 1516 Weinberg J described at [23] that the lien “confers upon the solicitor an equitable interest in the fruits of that litigation, provided that those fruits are gained, at least in part, as a result of the solicitor’s exertions on behalf of the client.” This appeared to adopt, as extracted by Weinberg J, what had been said by Lehane J in Roam Australia Pty Ltd v Telstra Corporation Ltd [1997] FCA 980 rather than what had been said by Mclelland CJ in Eq in Doyles Construction Lawyers v Harsands Pty Ltd (Unreported, Supreme Court of NSW, 24 December 1996) (“Doyles Construction”). Justice Lehane described as follows:
In each case, in my view, it must be a question whether the requisite causal link is established...on the evidence, to be regarded as brought about (or partially brought about) by the efforts of the solicitors...where solicitors have been actively involved over a considerable period in acting for a party to successful litigation, the conclusion is likely to follow that the solicitors have been instrumental in obtaining the result, or that the result is (at least in part) due to the solicitor’s efforts.
Chief Justice McLelland in Eq in Doyles Construction proposed a broader approach:
It is unnecessary for Doyles to demonstrate that the settlement came about as the result of specific efforts by them. According to the statement of principle [in Patience] ...it is sufficient to give rise to the equitable right that the settlement resulting in payment came about as a result of the legal proceedings and that the solicitor had acted for the client in those proceedings, this being treated as a sufficient causal link.”
The approach of Lehane J appears to connect findings and principle more closely than the assumption proposed by McLelland J and should be preferred, as it appears to have been by Weinberg J.
Prior to considering the circumstances further, this analysis excludes Ms OO’s claim. While the fact that Ms OO’s involvement was all pre litigation does not necessarily exclude a claim for a fruits of action lien, it is a factual circumstance that calls for greater clarity in demonstrating a connection to the result of the litigation. Ms OO’s evidence does not do so. There is little, if any, reason to consider that Ms OO’s involvement assisted the wife in her litigation.
Given the holding by AB Pty Ltd was the product of Ms OO’s unmeritorious claim upon the jewellery, Ms OO will be responsible for the associated costs of the jewellery’s retention there.
The claims for a fruits of litigation lien raised by each of AF Pty Ltd and ZZ Pty Ltd also rely on establishing a requisite connection between their efforts and the fruits of the litigation for the wife. Those claims necessarily rely upon the wife receiving, in the relevant sense, fruits. Whether she has done so, and whether a causal link to fruits is established, will be considered in the light of the outcome of the wife’s litigation as set out further below.

CONCLUDED PROPERTY POOL

The remnants of the property of the marriage can be described as follows.

The Suburb G proceeds

The Suburb G property proceeds subject to the Westpac mortgage, to the Mr AH mortgage, and to the B Pty Ltd mortgage are nil.
The husband asserted that the wife had retained $1.5 to $2 million worth of furniture from the Suburb G property. Neither the value, nor the wife’s retention of it was adequately supported by evidence, particularly in circumstances where the husband had the wife and children thrown out of the home, and denied access to it, by security on or about 1 May 2023.[41]

Suburb J property

This property is held by the wife, registered in her name. The wife holds, despite her assertions otherwise, the beneficial interest in this property as well as the legal title.
It is subject to a mortgage registered to D1 Pty Ltd. The sum secured by the mortgage is unidentified.
The gross value of the property is contended by the wife to be about $1 million. If retained by her, this assertion may be accepted as an admission against interest by the wife, subject then to a debt established by D1 Pty Ltd. Whether D1 Pty Ltd will be able to establish any debt, where they, as participants in these proceedings, chose not to secure that relief in these proceedings is yet to be seen. While it may well be the case that an Anshun estoppel prevents them from doing so, that is not a matter for this judgment.

Country P properties

As identified above, the husband holds numerous properties in Country P.
The husband previously, by his response and by his trial affidavit, asserted that he would transfer properties to the wife in Country P. He backtracked from this position in his oral evidence, and in his final Minute of Orders sought. There was almost no evidence as to the worth of the properties in Country P, the husband ascribing little value to them. It may be observed that evidence as to the nature of and manner of acquisition of those properties is peculiarly within the hands of the husband, who, resident in Country P, and with the control of those properties, is also in the best position to advance evidence as to the value of the properties.
Although the husband asserted that he is still married to his previous wife in Country P, rather than the wife in these proceedings and that, as a consequence of such, his previous wife has a right to fifty per cent of those properties and the wife has no right, no sufficient evidence to establish such was advanced by the husband, who again, it may be supposed, was well placed to source and to adduce such evidence. In this respect it may be observed that the husband asserted that he was able to provide such evidence “anytime you guys want me to present it” yet it was not produced. [42]
The failure to adduce such evidence is consistent with the husband’s claims to the court during the hearing that those properties are not a matter for this court.
The Country P properties are of indeterminate worth.

Jewellery

The wife asserts a value of about $702,000 (or $800,000 per her Outline of Case Document) to the jewellery that is held at AB Pty Ltd.[43]

DISCUSSION

The question that permeates the whole process of property adjustment pursuant to s 79 is one of whether it is just and equitable to make both any adjustment, and to make an adjustment in a particular manner.
The parties’ relationship commenced and continued in what superficially appeared as significant wealth but which, as is now revealed, was hollow.
For all the joint efforts of the parties during their relationship, and despite their prolific spending, lifestyle and acquisitions, all that remains is a narrow property pool, being comprised of the Country P properties, owned by the husband, the Suburb J property and the jewellery, owned by the wife.
Real property held in Country P is of an unknown value. That lack of evidence falls at the husband’s feet. That is a matter that undermines any claim that the husband might make for an adjustment of other property interest in his favour.
Further, the lack of confidence that the properties have any value or that in personam orders directed to the transfer of such properties would ever be met undermine the notion that an order directed to the transfer of that property would be of any benefit to the wife.
The wife holds property of a discerned value of about $1.7 million, comprised of the Suburb J property and the jewellery. Although the wife sought a transfer of the shareholdings in various corporate entities to herself, each was conditional upon an indemnity from the husband. The entities held no discernible value (including D1 Pty Ltd, in the absence of identification of the quantum of debt associated with the Suburb J mortgage). Any indemnity from the husband is worthless, and to the extent the wife also sought a general indemnity from the husband, such is not anchored in identification of that which requires indemnification. The wife’s application in this respect is without substantive merit.
Against the value of the property, the wife owes an unascertained but large amount of money to the various lawyers who have pursued what, when ultimately dug up, is an empty treasure chest. ZZ Pty Ltd holds a charge against the Suburb J property in their favour for their fees, whatever they might be.
The wife’s holding of the property falls against a background of her significant non-financial contributions to the welfare of the family being made by her both pre, and more substantively post separation. The wife also single handedly holds the responsibility for the care and maintenance of the parties’ children, the husband having failed in his responsibility for them, and giving no adequate assurance that he will in the future engage in the financial support of his children.
The broad sweep of these circumstances, in the context of what is individually held by the parties, does not lead to a conclusion that it is just and equitable to adjust the parties’ property interests at all.
Absent that conclusion, no adjustment is available, and the husband’s and wife’s competing applications fall to be dismissed.
The result will be the retention by the wife of the Suburb J property and the jewellery, and by the husband of the Country P properties. No declaratory relief in relation to the husband and wife in relation to the Suburb J or the Country P properties is warranted.
That outcome has consequences for the lien-based claims of each of the legal practices.
What remains to be considered is whether there is any relevant fruit for the lien to attach to. While on the one hand it could be said that the wife successfully resisted the husband’s claim for a property adjustment in his favour, on the other hand, the wife was unsuccessful in securing the object of her application, being an adjustment in her favour. Her claim for such was dismissed. The result of the litigation is that the wife merely retained what was hers, adjustment being refused. She neither obtained property, nor did she recover property, and she obtained no monetary judgment, unlike the circumstances in cases such as Firth. There is no judgment in her favour granting her relief. Under those circumstances I am not satisfied that there is relevant fruit of the litigation upon which a lien attaches, leaving ZZ Pty Ltd and AF Pty Ltd without that aspect of the relief that they seek.
That is not to suggest that they do not have an action in debt. They do not, however, have the benefit of a lien.
While various parties made various claims as to costs, reasonably, these matters were not prosecuted in the trial, given their connection to the outcome of the trial. Any issues of costs will fall to be determined in the usual manner.

REGULATORY REFERRAL

A last matter remains. During the proceedings I identified to Ms OO that I was considering her referral to the NSW legal regulator. During the proceedings Ms OO repeatedly sought to adduce evidence of her interactions with the wife that, should her assertion that she was the wife’s lawyer be accepted, as it has, meant that she was obliged, unless an exception was identified, to maintain the confidentiality of those communications. No exception was identified. Those actions by Ms OO warrant a referral, and orders will be made to facilitate such, including to afford the regulator with access both to the transcripts and the documents filed by Ms OO.

CONCLUSION

Orders will be made refusing the wife’s application for relief, but declaring her right to the jewellery and facilitating its return to her possession.
The husband’s application for relief will be refused.
B Pty Ltd will be granted the relied as ultimately sought by it.
Ms OO’s application will be dismissed.
AF Pty Ltd’s application will be dismissed.
ZZ Pty Ltd’s application will be dismissed but for a declaration that it holds a charge in respect of the Suburb J property.
All other extant applications will be dismissed.
